the gadfly of area 51 was a Non Entity (NE). that was how he had been classified by Science and that was how he was known to the commanding officer of the phenomon division, Bradley Bradley.
asked why he been named Bradley twice, he said it was because his twin had died in the womb, strangled by the umbilical cord. 

"and twins are-" the gadfly hummed. 

"two." Bradley held up his thumb and forefinger. "Two children."

"and even so. odd as it is." the gadfly posed a question: "happens?"

"happens what? do you mean how did it happen? It was due to complications."

the gadfly intuited this was false, by pressing his "digits" to a small pocket of "skin" near the nape of his "neck".

being a non entity, he couldnt be said to have any of these things in the actual sense, "skin," "neck", any of it. he hadn't been diagrammed yet. he was on list, as were all the other donors in northnortheast wing, that was situated two miles underground in Florida somewhere. the entrance to that wing of the complex was a fountain in a derelict mall, guarded night and day by hired vagrants. 

interestingly and entirely coincidentally, an initiative was being headed up by the mayor of the florida town to stop urban exploration of old structures, like the mall. children were warned in no uncertain terms to stay away from these dangerous places. a youtube channel had gone mysteriously dark after venturing into that mall, and his 700 subscribers were beginning to ask questions. 

but that was no business of his, Bradley Bradley said, or of anyone else outside the Pressing Matters division. this was the Phenomenon Division; their primary and only concern was that of (1) recieving classifications from Science (which was southeast wing, [point of ingress: a favela in Rio de Janeiro, to give a sense of the scope of the complex]) and (2) to phenomonize those classifications into something coherent or incoherent, as the case required. 

"so if" he gestured with his cigarette, "if I were to phenonomize twins and what befell my twin, I'd start with that, with 'twin.' first the word, and then the surrounding words, 'brother,' 'cord'. Lay out the schema. and then you start drawing lines." he drew a zig-zag of smoke. "kind of like you, NE."

the gadfly hummed knowingly. depending on the light, the NE had been discribed variously as looking like "loosely tied-together sticks the color of air that jangled around" and "fracture lines in a window" and "a fucking waste of taxpayer money."

sometimes, at a half glance, he would take on the form of a child, a small boy, but the image never held at direct eyepoint, and was not consistent enough to merit even a class 2 (sub-certain) or 3 (sub-sub-certain) classification. 

much resented by Science for this fact, or lack of fact, the gadfly was "put" on a bullet tram to northnortheast, and had spent the better part of a decade in purgatory with the donors. 

typically, the donors were down-and-outs or pariahs of other sectors with no particular use; they got an amenities card, a cot, and free rein of the wing. When they were ready or there was an express need, they would be euthanized, scalpelized, and studied. 

as first-of-kind, the gadfly was a shoe-in for donorship; the board was over the moon when they got word from Science about the transfer. 

several members of the DPB--an initialism that, it was assumed, had at one time stood for something, likely Donor Program Board, but which had become through long and careless use become a kind of shorthand, "dee-pub," for any toothless governing body with more policies than power--went down to the tram station in their caps and scrubs to welcome him with smiles that turned quickly to frowns as the new arrival emerged, flitted, conveyed itself toward them, blinking coyly in and out of the perceptual.

they hadn't been informed. they'd been taken for a ride. "unprecedented," Science had promised, "characteristics rarely seen and barely known."

barely known because barely knowable, it soon became clear. no sacs or glands, no limbs or organs, none of the anatomical abnormalities that made scalpelists' hearts flutter. 

for christ's sake, the thing had no discernable properties at all, except the stray glimpse of his lines and cracks, and--I don't believe this has been mentioned yet--a relentless, precocious, infuriating, and deeply uncomfortable sense of being questioned, not interrogated, but questioned at the most fundamental level and being found wanting. 

a small, active, immaterial presence. like a thousand soft fingers fretting at your edges, pulling at strands. devoid of malice, but smug, coquetteish. think a child's plaintive "why?" reduced to an ultra-concentrate and dripped down your ear, a warm itch that spreads and coats the insides.

"I feel," one of the welcome party remarked, scratching at his surgical cap, "--ridiculous." (He would later leave the scalpel behind for a whistle and crew socks, accepting a comfortably underpaid post as a calisthenics instructor in Berkley-3.)

as for the Chief Scalpelist, he was miffed beyond belief when, upon examining the papers that Science had (quite intentionally) withheld up to this point, he saw the clever bit of shiftiness those bastards in Rio had pulled: "determination: undetermined [rescinded]; re-determination: non-entity; recommended for relocation to donor-holding pending developments."

Science knew good and well of course that donation was contingent upon phenomenizing, which was itself contingent on classifying. absent both a class and a phenomenological dossier, the gadfly was effectively a ward of the wing until he decided to die or reconstitute or whatever the equivalent for him would be, at which the DPB would have a death on their hands with no research data to show for it. 

there would be no Declaration of Find, no juicy postmortem, no notability statistics for inclusion in the quarter's register, no visit from biotechnics with all the fanfare that entailed, and worst of all, none of the attendant funditure. instead, there would just be this nagging presence, this unflagging nuisance, offering nothing but questions and demanding nothing but answers. 

no one knew quite how to proceed. they tried, certainly, by every known method and channel to find recourse, some squirmy little loophole or sliver of precedent. relay after relay was sent, each less passively and more actively agressive than the last, first to Science in a direct attempt to get them to clean up their mess (radio silence); then to Appellation Appeals to see if some magic could be worked there to get the NE "named," which proved to be a dead end as well. the Chief's aide even tried--"entirely of his own volition," the official report anxiously clarified--to terminate the gadfly with a laser tool. it didn't work; the aide was fired.

there was no getting around it, there was no getting at it. the gadfly was here to stay.

The Elementary Particle: Sex, Space, Science and God

The presence of God, a great distance away, gave form, substance, and meaning to the world and--separately--to our place in it. attempts to reach him were geographical, navigational, pilgrimages across treacherous moral terrain, too vast to be traversed in a single lifetime, and so we must propagate, make more of ourselves. but in so doing, we increase the distance, reinscribing the mutative influence of our carnal nature, losing whatever ground we gained.

then came materialism, it sucked God from the sky, an ancient aborted fetus, leaving a gaping womb, an impersonal void, eternal and unspeakably vast. we had nowhere to go, and nothing to be, and yet mechanisms of desire were still at work in us. we wandered but with no destination, we fucked and bred but with no great hope to pass on.

the emergence of positivism grounded us somewhat; it still operated within the materialist framework in that what was "real" still lay beyond the human, but it not inexorably out of reach. it was at our fingertips, in our noses and eyes. the primacy of sensation gave us permission to be small bodies in great spaces and gave purpose to the interchange: to know the void, to sense it, measure it. a reason to fuck.

but there was Einstein; he troubled the waters, suggesting that reason by way of experience, though reliable enough on a human scale, falters at both the atomic and cosmic levels. it does not constitute an absolute, nothing does, only the hard limit of lightspeed. we dissolved again, reduced to the elementary, positive of nothing.

nevertheless, things happen, we see them, we know them; we "act as if," desperately, subjectified within an inch of our lives. sex becomes the only way to reclaim some sense of objectness, to exert control, however illusory, over time (becoming young again through offspring) and over distance (pulling the lover close, two as one). it becomes the new religion, sex does, offering a unitive vision wherein we are the gods, larger than ourselves, pulsing, generative. the void become but a space for ever more ridiculous tantric permutations. 

this does not hold, cannot hold. and, says the quantum physicist (troubling believer, positivist and hippie alike) the reason is that _there is nothing that is not observed_, no God in heaven, no infinite void, no nirvana, no final mort, petit or otherwise. once we see it, it is. until then, it is not. quantum states can be predicted, certainly, with an extraordinary degree of precision, but only if one is able to divest oneself of cherished notions of something beyond, to collapse the eternal yardstick against which man has always held himself, proudly measuring out his heroisms, his sacrifices, his romances, his dreams, down to the stifling constraints of the immediate field of view.

big pills are no problem, we will choke them down happily, asphyxiate with a smile on our face. but this one, the quantum pill, was far too small. and that, Houellebecq implicitly suggests, is why we are miserable. 

we are mutants in the sewer of desire, dreaming of a life above ground beneath a boundless sky. but of course we cannot get there until we relinquish the desire to get there.  if we could but observe a new human, ready made, beyond sex and the ravages of time, perhaps it would be so.

there is no romance* left in nature; photography tamed it. there is no romance left in sex; therapy tamed it. there is no romance left in the Digital; google tamed it. there isn't even any romance left in revolution; marx tamed it. art? there's no spirit in it. religion? there's no art in it.

narcoleptic, anhedonic, we languish in dreams of the bygone, foregone, orgone.

the only romance left is pain--mute, bare, individual. we must ravish ourselves with the whips and chains of _hyperawareness_, set ourselves to the path of the enlightened sadist, bodhisattvas of pain

*romance is the idea of the human in the more-than-human AND of the more-than-human in the human. it flows both way, mystically, "naively." anything that attempts to singly unhumanize the human (models) OR to humanize the more than human (morals) works against romance.

UNION
They are seated in the restaurant at two tables pushed together, the after-church crowd, the quiet clamor.
Grandpa is at table's head, a heavy-set, tanned man with receding grey on top and an air of weary wellbeing. Grandma to his right in Sunday best, beaming vaguely around at everyone. "Oh now," she chides her two nondescript grandchildren, boy and girl, who are spitting at each other across the table and kicking each other under it. 
The children's father, heir apparent and beneficiary of pater's hairline, sits there with a put-on scowl, as if to say "I dislike this restaurant, my bad children, all of it, the whole rotten deal."
He looks sidelong at his wife--another object of his infinite distate--appraises her flat hair, pert nose, twitchy mouth. Still pretty, he concedes, in an average sort of way. 
Bringing her home the first time (begrudged reminiscence), she wore a loud african necklace that did not suit her at all, and kept fussing with it anxiously. "Nice enough gal" was the verdict and so they were married. But now--charmless, harmless, unadorned; she has made children and done her part. She neither likes nor dislikes anything.
There's a sudden spike in chatter, everyone talking at once, then a silence. They all look to grandpa, who is looking up at a dusty ceiling fan wobbling precariously above them. He finds himself unable to tear his gaze away, something about its lazy elliptical motion. Strands of cobweb waving.
"Ah." He shakes his head. "Let's uh-" he puts out his right hand to his wife and his left to his son, who sighs.
Hands are joined; the table is joined. "Son, will you do the honors."
Sighs again. "Dear Father," he mumbles.
The server approaches now, pad in hand. She notices too late and bows her head awkwardly. "Bless this food, bless our time together." She peeks at the children peeking at her. She has two kids of her own, and a dead husband. Dead to her at least.
"Amen." A hurried chorus. 
"Ahem. So what can I get you."
The adults order coffee, decaf for the nervous wife; the children get juice. "We're out of that." They get a different kind of juice.
Grandpa's eyes return to the fan--why the damn attraction. He smiles, knowing that his son is casting surly glances at him. Must maintain the grandfatherly air: beneficient, absent, not long for this world but not short for it either. 
He doesn't like church that much himself, really. The hymns and luncheons and sanctimonious fuss. But there's a rhythm that has to be maintained, at all costs, and sunday is the downbeat, the vital cue. Nations rise or fall on this, this ritual. Anyways, where does his son get off on the adolescent crap, the damn moodiness. Fatherhood should have wrung that out of him by now. 
"Someone should clean that fan," he finally says. 
"Oh yes" says his wife, looking up. She pats his hand. "Someone should."
He grunts and smiles again. The children are flicking juice. "Quit it with that," the son snaps.  
"Hey now. Let's keep it in the glass." "Yes grandpa." Cooler heads prevail.
A table over, a lone man is reading a paper and eating collards. "Who still reads papers," the son thinks enviously, wiping flecks off his tie. "And who eats collards." He'd always been a picky eater, to his mother's consternation and his father's shame. Too bad he wasn't a pickier mate. 
"Quit it with that," he says again, viciously. It's unclear who's being addressed. 
There's a loud clatter from the kitchen, a dropped tray. The restaurant turns around, eager for a glimpse at the offender.
"This is the most we can hope for," thinks grandpa, watching the drama fold and unfold. "The best of all possible worlds." Was that Descartes? Liebniz?
Their server comes by to check on them, orders are placed: burger, burger, steak, salad, nothing for me thanks. Could you open the window blinds? She does.
Outside, the noon sun bakes over the treeless lot, heat waves off the car hoods. He could feel it already--the sting of the door handle, the interior swelter, irate twist of the AC knob. He's driving away now, bumping down that familiar stretch of bad road, well under the posted limit, eyes aren't what they used to be. Already home now, lowering into the recliner, pulling off shoes.  Performative grunts and groans, patting around for the clicker. Game's on; game's over; time for bed.
The glint of a car brings him out of reverie. It moves past the window slowly, the driver's dark shape barely visible, neck craned for an open spot. Something strange about all this. 
The children go to the bathroom out of boredom. Food comes while they're gone. Familiar choreographies. Pass the ketchup, pass the--oh. Hey stop running. Yes, that's what you ordered. I don't care, you're going to eat it. 
Grandpa stirs his second cup of coffee, a rising sense of nausea. That same car again, circling. Fat drops on the windshield, a sunshower out of nowhere. 
How's the food. Great. Good. You've barely touched it. Here try this. Refills?
Thump....thump, thump. A skipped beat, a stab of dread. Impossible. Clutch at the pain point. Thump, thump thump thump
No, no ice please. You get more that way. Plus, bacteria. I read an article. 
Periphery coming in and out of focus now, sine waves humming. Someone is talking about the Sickness, their cousins in Alabama, a court date. We finished with those plates? 
"What's taking so long," he gasps, growls.
"Bob, what's wrong. Bobby."
Rising to his feet, a cornered bear, upsetting dishes: "We need to go. Get the check."
The children look up from play, awestruck. 
"Dad, sit down." A note of concern puncturing practiced scorn. People are looking.
"Where's our damn server."
"Bob, sit down now. Language, the kids. What's wrong, Bobby."
He lurches to the window, presses his hands against it. It's a downpour now, a pall. Somewhere in it is that car, the shrouded menace, circling circling, slow and unhurried. A spot will open up, a spot always opens up.  
"Damn you" he hisses. 
"Language Bob!"
He has to get out there, into the rain. He bumps into the server, inevitable. "Did you need more coffee, sir?"
Between tables and chairs, through all that nauseating wriggling motion, a wounded stagger; distance grows and shrinks; the door is a mile off. This has never happened to anyone, he thinks. I'm the first to walk here, along this pattern of tiles. 
Tinkle of the bell, he bursts out the door with animal force; contact at last; he's made it. Under the eaves of the awning, feeling the splatter on his hands, he sees straight again, somewhat. Augustine's concussions--that's a pun he would have made back there, back then, 20 years or minutes ago--helmet to helmet with God. He would have chuckled at that.
The rain stops as suddenly as it began, spent; the ringing subsides.
Is this is how it goes in the final moments? A letting up, a lessening? The delayed anticlimax; surrender? Come on, now. You're fine, old man. You wear the armor of a thousand thousand Sundays.
Trembling, he pulls a mint from his pocket, unwraps it, places it on his tongue like a holy coal. As a kid, you'd bite it, crunch it, pick the bits from your molars. But with time, one learns the pleasures of the long game, sucking things down to a thin wafer. 
Inhale. Exhale. There's steam rising from the asphalt, that hot after-rain smell. Plink, plink in the downspout. Everything is still. The car is gone.
Hah. Leibniz, you old fool. He smiles, then grunts. What an embarrassing episode. Something to wince at and never talk about. His son's little smirk. God. 
He spits the mint out in the flowers and goes back in, sheepishly. The restaurant air hits cold against wet, clinging fabric. It gives him an expansive feeling, a wide yawning in the chest, like waking up early with nothing to do that day and all the time to do it.

clarity (hexagram 30), clinging fire below clinging fire.
not a clinging outward but a clinging inward.

fire in the sky and fire in the core
a docile cow enmeshed between, not luminous herself, but a benficiary of luminescence, a maker of milk.
the young cling to her udders

Text:
talcum (inspired by Raising Arizona)
dont spurn the blue intangible
dont grow too fast
dont gas the car on the tar smelling highway
or flash metal at circumstance

climb the ladder rung on rung
slow as cactus
and dont steal love from television
it runs off electric
parallel to sewer lines

furnish yourself better
with a prototype dream 
in wide open spaces
like a lizard or the desert hare

Text:
BRAINFOOD
I want to think about ways that the workings of Mind and Thought might be mapped to and brought into analogical relation with the biological processes of ingestion, digestion, and excretion beyond mere notions of  input/output. 
First to the food or meat. This is raw sensation or unprocessed data, sought out and hunted down by the Nose, the Hands, the Eyes. It is brought up to Mouth, the oral/cranial Cavity which serves as point of egress and site of consumption. 
Teeth are words, some blunt some incisive, a set of living bones and received wisdoms (prone to irregularity, discoloration, shifting, and decay), with which we have been endowed to tear, grind, and chew the whole sensation into its component parts, reassembled by the Salivary function (Desire) into a softened mass or Bolus suitable for Tasting on the tongue of emotion and swallowing via the spasmodic impulse or downward thrust (force of will)
Here our food enters the deep intestinal labyrinth, that Unconscious interior wherein the involuntary act of Digestion takes place, disintegrating the received sensation on its molecular level, extracting through various enzymatic processes the nutritive psychic materials that enliven the whole man, and expelling the nonessential elements as waste. 
And here, distasteful as it may seem, we are forced to draw comparison between creative output and that of excretion. The analogy, perhaps, does not hold insofar as the creative act often generates or possesses beautiful qualities while the defecatory rarely does; both, however, do require effort, hold perverse fascinations, and can be indicative of one s general health whether physical or psychic.

CALISTHENICS
social kinesthetics/calisthenics - toward a systematic theory or proactive regime of collective "movement" - exercising the body politic through controlled motions (queues, fast, war, carnival) and at regular intervals (once in 7, thrice in 12) so that--in time--we might all groan a little less and spring a little more beneath the shared weight of Socius

CARTOON
modern medias worst crime is that it has cartoonized and trivialized power. this is fine when it is framed comedically (as in Looney Toons) or when the dissonance is played for laughs; it is downright insidious when it is not (as in YA and marvel). people and children especially have a developed a distorted understand of great strength or bravery or intellect as a thing with which one is endowed or not endowed rather than something that one works to obtain and express.

CLOTHESPIN
society as sprung mechanism, the dual prongs of which operate on principles of torsion and tension to do their unitive work, i.e. a fastening/pinning of cultural linens to the ideological line.
By lever action, the dichotomous prongs are brought to bear upon one another--male upon female, order upon chaos, life upon death.
Pinching the prongs closed at one end pries them open at the other, like a reluctant jaw, which--once released--clamps down on and secures life's billowy materials against the fleeting winds of time and impulse and chance.
So we come to see that the social mechanism, like the clothespin, can be considered a useful tool only insofar as it retains an inborn resistance to external force, figured as the coiled fulcrum. 
Take, for example, the unconscious mind and those bodies of ritual practice which have purported to heal and to reveal it, from the shamanistic traditions of prehistory to the psychoanalysis of the modern day.
Let us call the one prong framework and the other freeplay.
By what means are these opposing impulses brought into a working social relationship so as to pin the psyche down or air it out?
Where does the fulcrual point lie (if anywhere), and how is the tension stored there (if at all)?
The answer, I propose, is primarily socio-ontological, encompassing both sense of self and sense of place. 
Which is to say that--in the past at least--all healing practice was intended to propagate and reinforce a shared sense of collective identity, a social self, manifested through tribal affiliation and tied inextricably to geographical location. 
Exclusivity supplies the tension necessary to pin or fasten.
As time went on, however, and the exclusive barriers dissolved, the ontic force exerted upon the spring proved more than the mechanism could bear, collapsing the three part structure, snapping the taut line, and reducing the ordered triangle (prong-coil-prong) to a decentered jumble. Hence the dryer drum menality of the psychoanalyst and "new age" nitwit, therapy as centrifuge, preoccupation with the individual point rather than the relational vertices.
Of course, this metaphor--like the clothespin itself--is limited in scope--an inch or less of play. 
But the play works. It suffices. 
While the industrial unit tumbles and degrades, consuming coin and excreting lint within the purgatorial confines of the laundrymat, the simple sprung pin maintains the quiet dignity of the article and its wearer, acknowleging and operationalizing the vicissitudes of an errant breeze.

CODEMONKEY
the capabilties of humans and their systems are inversely related. as systems grow in scope and sophistication, so too does our reliance upon them and our ignorance of them, such that, in time, they can no longer be said to belong to us but us to them. 
it is self-induced regression on a massive scale: our epochal return to the childlike state of neo-animistic primitivism, cogs in machines we forgot we made. (see the IT custodian, the codemonkey--intermediary states in the deevolutionary process)

CONDUITS
spacetime is a conduit for subject-objects (you, me) which are in turn conduits for communicational frequencies or energies.
the dynamic nature of these nested channels precludes claims of ownership or fault.
which is to say, you cannot "waste" time or materials, since you do not in fact possess them, but merely move through and around them, just as energies move through and around you.
conduct your energies openly and gently toward the subject-objects that occupy the shared spatiotemporal stream, and the kindness will be returned in form of synchronicities, unimpeded flows.
misdirect your energies, however, and blockages will form, collisions will occur, all manner of snags and entanglements. 
[take, for instance, the man on his morning commute; he honks and curses; he musn't be late. 
his relationship with space and time is an adversarial one. he believes he must contend with/exert himself upon the former (space) in order to gain or save the latter (time).
this is false.
the man does have agency, but not of the sort he imagines. in the realm of the clock and the carburetor, we are all of us as helpless as stones. we have neither the obligation nor indeed even the ability to "get places" "on time," to make the material world bend to our will.
for all his wild attempts to accelerate, to maneuver, to escape the gridlock, the man does not and cannot move of his own accord; rather, he is moved by forces beyond him. 
if we would but realize this, come to see ourselves not as originating agents but as dynamic conduits in our own right, begin reaching out energetically to the other nodes in the network, accomodating them and communicating with them to our fullest capacity, we would find ourselves soon arriving wherever it is we are intended to be, equipped with whatever materials we are intended to have, carried along effortlessly by the currents we once vainly strove to fight.
so what i propose is reeorientation toward a meta-/co-conductive paradigm wherein the following holds true:
that if we concern ourselves mainly with the relational/communicational (Who-Why-How), the temporal/material (What-Where-When) will sort itself out in our collective favor.

CORDS*
The importance of internal  cord management,  i.e. wrapping up or ziptying your mental/emotional slack those lengths of excess cordage to avoid psychic snags, material entanglements, and obstructions to internal visibility/traceability

COUNTRY
mapping the generic parameters of country music onto a cartesian plane wherein the x-axis stretches across the American continent, west to east (from the cowboy to coast), while y-axis functions socioeconomically, up and down, from the collegiate heights of neo-folk to the depths of deep-south destitution.

CONTRACT
a contract implies a business relationship entered into freely and consensually to the (one would hope) mutual benefit of both parties; however, in the case of what is often called the "social contract," this appears not to be the case, as one is born into a state of compulsory societal participation with no real opportunity to renew or reaffirm the bond that was established by prior generations on their behalf or, increasingly, to ther detriment. it is almost as if the social contract functions less in the business sense (consensually signing a set of stipulations) and more in the epidemiological sense (as in contracting a virus or some state-altering condition), perhaps even in the covenantal sense (Noahic/Abrahamic promise, "your seed and their seed").

CREED
And let these principles be my guide: to live simply. a spare life, characterized by economy and straightforwardess. to live contentedly. a joyful life, characterized by acceptance and appreciation of the smallest thing. to live spiritually. a deep life, characterized by a sense of connection and higher purpose.

CYBERSPACES*
4chan   an insular population of young, embittered, disaffected and/or maladjusted males perpetuating niche board cultures characterized by technological literacy, performative aggression, reflexive contrarianism, pedantic one-upmanship, gallows-humor schadenfreude, and tongue-in-cheek infantility calculated to repulse the uninitiated 
Twitter   a splintered intersectional warzone of over-socialized manic depressives/narcissists attempting to curate, mimic, and maintain unique and unassailable brands usually in the form of  hot takes  and meta-takes that walk the razor s edge of sociopolitical currency and self-aware parody, all the while militantly defending their brands from perceived threats with rapid fire exchange of invective, feigned detachment, intellectual posturing, passive aggressive side swipes, ironic hyper-referentiality, and a general sense of libidinous abandon.
Tumblr   a playground for neurotic femininity in all its obsessive adornment, mutually effusive affirmation, virtue signaling, cliquish gatekeeping, and deep resentment of any sort of masculine intrusion upon the codified bodies of textual stylistics, practiced sarcasm, layered in-jokes, pop cultural hyper literacy, and unspoken hierarchies of exchange 
Facebook   an over commercialized wasteland of digital illiterates dealing in overinflated memetic currencies with no fixed exchange rate
Reddit   downvote

GAME
I am compelled toward battlefield (and video games in general) by a combination of psychic drives including: competitive/exhibitive (desire to take the field against human opponents, incentivized toward optimal performance by well designed rulesets and clearly defined win/loss conditions), acquistional/completionist (desire to accumulate and showcase an inventory of honorifics, adornments, and items of relative rarity/finitude obtained through skill, persistence, and dumb luck), aggressive (desire to do violence to others and risk violence to self in pursuit of larger and justifiable aims), fraternal (desire to inhabit, participate in, and become recognized throughout communal/competitive spaces with delineated hierarchies and status indicators), and comedic/playful (desire to generate and appreciate situational tensions between expectation and instantiation with the advantage of distance and low-stake involvement).
As these drives have little to no expression in my primary realities, they compel me toward secondary realities, figured as the simulated battlefield, which is all the more enticing as it offers many of the chemical/instinctual/social satisfactions of warfare without the survival risks typically entailed thereby (the preservational drive)

HANDLES*
Greek Stoic Epictetus spoke of a handle by which an event may be lifted, and a handle by which it cannot.
Put another way, every occurrence may be approached in one of two ways:
      as an infringing (of external upon internal) OR
      as an opening (for the internal to better understand or interact with the external)
If you miss the winning lottery ticket by one number you can see this as an infringing (of random chance upon your ordered desires) or as an opening (for you to better appreciate the absurdities of a world beyond your control).
Do not lift by the constraint, but by the constrainer. Rather than focusing on that which has been done or not done toward or against you, focus on the doer and your relationship to it.
Do not grasp the nearly won lottery by having been denied, grasp it by the denier chance or fate or the God-Who-Is-There a force or being to whom you owe all things, both your misfortunes and your fortunes.
Do not grasp the untimely termination of your employment by having been fired, grasp it by having been employed by an employer from whom things were learned and to whom a degree of thanks (however slight) is owed.
So says Epicetus. And so say I, with one notable qualification: 
Further examples:
      If I drop a glass on accident and become angry, what causes that anger? The anger comes from the fact there are external acting which constrain my ability to do what I want or to not do what I don t want. I m focused on the constraining, on my own subjectivity which is lacking.
      If I drop a glass and feel nothing, what causes my apathy? The apathy comes from the fact that that there is an internal lacking which precludes my ability to want anything insofar as I am nothing. I m focused on the lack of constraint
All this is well and good, Epictetus; however, I propose that there is a third handle, a third way Joint Use.
In other words, there is not one right handle and one wrong one, but rather two handles which must both be used in order to lift the object without spillage.

GILETTE*
As the application of warm water to the face opens pores, easing the scrape of the razor on skin, so does the warmth of a kind word prepare the spirit for the blade of criticism and life s sharp encounters

GREY'S*
an anatomical theory of system: the skeletal frame (mechanics and infrastructures), the connective tissue and tendons (logistics), the striated bundle (mission and manpower), the Skin or Face (rhetorics and optics)

HOBBYWHORES
hobbyism is dreadful, odious. mild mannered hordes of middle age castrati buying overpriced starter kits, proofreading replies to noone on dying internet forums. I get nausea from it: their sense of dutiful, put on industriousness, of practiced curiosity, of sallow well being. 
pursue the thing with mad, singular, obsessive abandon; or else do it sullenly, begrudingly, poorly. do it because you must or not at all. drop the farce. i address myself.

IRONY
The ironic or sarcastic turn is a performative, deformative mode/code of signifying one s facility with and freedom from a set of conventions (linguistic, cultural, etc.) by consciously negating or transgressing them. Instantiated in what we might call  knowing infractions  (syntactic slights/slides, semantic winks, referential nods, mimetic gestures, dismissive tones), the ironic act is just that an act but one so subtle as to exclude, confuse, and escape the uninitiated majority while also establishing a tacit solidarity with anyone else keen enough to detect and appreciate the sly subversion. It s a kind of mating call, repulsing the unsuitable and attracting those with similar aptitudes for and attitudes toward the socio-communicational  games we play,  in all of their nuanced constructed-ness. 
In this way, irony functions against itself a deflation that inflates, a lessening that enlarges. Practiced distance becomes a unifying force within this formulation, a brotherhood of detachment and anti-sentiment. Interface is rendered Artifice (art of face). Ego presides at the expense of the superego that engendered it. 
Simply put: irony is breaking the rules to show how well you know them (and how little you care about them). Its utility is anti-utility: dependence upon the very thing it derides.
For example, when I call a bad catch  nice,  I do not mean that it is actually nice I mean that it was bad but only within the arbitrary rulesets of a game that doesn t deserve sincere effort or consideration. To call a bad catch  nice  is worse than calling it bad, insofar as it undermines not only the skill of the player but the legitimacy of the game itself and the language around it. The implication: I can do better with ease than you can do with effort (if I cared to, which I don t). Whether or not this is true is of no consequence only that it was expressed in as offhand a way as possible. Offhandedness is key. The moment you let up your practiced air of never having practiced, the game is over (and real games, fun games can finally resume).
But no one wants to lose, and no wants to look as if they want to win. Like a sickness, irony spreads. Ease turns to dis-ease, moving with feverish coolness among the unbothered youth, a second plague, a Black Undeath, a pox that is contracted in isolation and transmitted through lack of genuine contact symptomatic of nothing, critical of everything, terminally dormant. 
Eventually, however, a kind of herd immunity is reached. In a society inoculated against sincerity, there is nothing left for the ironic man to scorn, no failed attempts above which to elevate himself, only an infinite regression of hollow conventions and post-conventions to remove one s self from.
So we invent new horrors to bore us, new fears to yawn at behind our mandated face coverings. And this, I propose, is the true pandemic: not the germ, not the real and living virus, but the inhuman and unsmiling masks we wear against it.

PROGRESS
Working theory: every major cultural movement of the past two centuries commonly called upon as evidence of social progress and growth is in fact evidence to the contrary insofar as it subversively promotes (and indeed originates from) the elite attempt not to  better the world  or any such nonsense but rather to consolidate power (e.g. by destabilizing the family unit through women s lib), massify opinion (e.g. indoctrinating the youth with a curriculum of  tolerance  and  open-mindedness  through mandatory public education that renders them increasingly susceptible to propaganda), delocalize identity (e.g. racializing issues of local sovereignty and affiliation, except where these affiliations pose no threat [i.e. minorities]), increase surveillance (e.g. establishing psychological and technological mechanisms of censorship and enforcement against  hate speech  [read: the crime of critical individual thought] in the name of  promoting civil discourse ) and control population growth (e.g. discouraging procreation through celebration of  alternative  same-sex lifestyles and reproductive rights). 
It has nothing to with  basic human rights  and everything to with base human desires, specifically the desire for unfettered power and control.
[NOTE: I am not saying that any of these things are  bad  as such simply that they can be traced, I think convincingly, to ulterior motives that are incongruent with the alleged altruism/lofty humanism of the progressive agenda.]

TOOL
Toward a deanthropized theory of tool: Molar instrumentality, machinic recursion, and Theseus 
We tend to think of the natural world as resource, as raw material, as merely instrumental in the grand human project of bringing-about or bringing-forth   and a bringing-forth of what? of the inmost possibility, the forgotten knowledge, the energetic flows residing not within the earth (as mineral ore) but within ourselves (as infinite store).
We cut down the tree that we might fashion the parts that we might construct the instruments that we might coax ourselves out upon them as song or word or vision and thereby come to know ourselves. Thus nature s value seems not to be inherent but rather derived from its instrumental role in allowing man to bring himself about. 
But what if this line of thought were to be reversed? What if nature is the agent, the active entity, and we merely its medium? 
The spirit of the tree seeks passage; it calls to the man and uses the man; it uses his hands as tools and his tools as tools; it makes itself Boat thereby and grants us berth as payment.
I.e.: man as that which the tree operationalizes in the process of making itself known to itself as The-Sailing-Thing or The-Singing-Thing. What if we are not Theseus, as we imagine, but rather his ship, our capacities the sails, swapped out selectively by naiads and dryads until the perfect instrumental configuration is reached? 
And within this recursive reimagining, we come now to see our illusions of prime agency functioning merely as teeth on the saw or cogs on the gear to better enable the enabling of Nature coming forth (as man coming forth [as nature coming forth]).
What then? The man, the tree, the ship: instruments that craft their makers, tuning the tuner at pitches more or less to their liking, more or less to their likeness.

ANOTHER ROUND
Four jaded middle age high school teachers decide to test the hypothesis that humans are born with a BAC deficit of 0.05, adopting on an ostensibly experimental basis Hemingway s famous habit of daylong alcohol consumption with abstinence on nights and weekends. At first, the effects are positive, with noticeable improvements in professional performance and interpersonal connection, both with family and students, but as addiction sets in, their project spirals out of control, with Martin losing his wife, Tommy losing his job, and the group of friends losing Tommy (to, it is implied, suicide), after a brief period of relative normalcy and sobriety.
Wallowing in his grief, Martin seems poised for a headlong dive back into his old ways when he receives a text from his wife indicating a desire to reunite, setting the stage for the admittedly very cool dance sequence where mikkelsen prances through a spray of champagne before the closing freeze frame of his spontaneous leap into the bay. 
The film feels, to put it kindly, a bit maudlin, the premise a bit contrived. Even the reference to Kierkegaard, dropped during the oral exam of an anxiety-ridden student (who, by the way, passes the exam on his teacher's suggestion to  liquor up  beforehand) comes off as forced. 
However hamfisted it may be in execution, the film does gesture toward some interesting thematic interplays of anxiety, sociality, and sexuality, all bound up in alcohol as Libation/Liberation, the Effusive Spirit, the celebratory/revelatory communion that compels us as people and as a species toward our individual and collective leaps into the great unknown where loss and possibility reside hand in hand.

HITCHERHIKER'S GUIDE (AND JUNG)
Recently watched the old BBC adaptation of Adams  Hitchhiker s Guide. Comically low budget, but charming in its way. 
The miniseries pairs nicely with Jung s Undiscovered Self, wherein religious experience is framed as the counterbalance to the mass mindedness of state control.
On the side of freedom stands the individuated psychic encounter with and experience of symbol-made-real, word-made-flesh on the other, a rote credal conformance to the secularized banalities of statistical average. 
While Adams would seem, on the surface, to belong to that school of smug pop atheism/scientism (a la Dawkins/Hitchens), glibly disabusing the reader-viewer of any ridiculous notions about mankind s privileged place in the universe (imagine it! A talking ape! On a speck called Earth!), he also doesn t having anything particularly nice to say about the prevailing power structures that we simian speck-dwellers have constructed in place of the god we killed.
The bureaucratic incompetence displayed by his petty officials, both on the terrestrial and intergalactic level, seem to suggest not just a distaste for specific instances of human backwardness, but a genuine scorn for all manmade Systems superstitious or otherwise. You can sense the malicious glee beneath his matter of fact description of Earth s unceremonious destruction part of a highway project no less, obviously paralleling the planned demolition of protagonist Arthur Dents home at the hands of the city council.
On this point, I think Jung and Adams strike a similar note. Hitchhiker is above all a panegyric to the absurd, the outr ,  chaos at feast  or at play.
The titular encyclopedia within the world of the novel is a wildly inaccurate and imaginative text that sparks in its readers a sense of the danger, variety, and lunacy of the world around them.
While he may demythologize/divest the Man of supernatural trappings, he is still invested deeply in perhaps not the intrinsic value but at least the aleatory and bizarre qualia of individual/universal experience.
I think Jung, while certainly placing a much higher priority upon preservation of symbolic/mythic heritage, would at least have a chuckle at the Vogon bureaucrats and maybe even find evocations of sacred metaphor in the Guide s representational liberties i.e. things are other than they seem. 
In conclusion: Don t panic. The world as you think you know it may vaporize, the infrastructure crumble around you, the buttresses of mass opinion fall away, but the self remains a raw strange thing, undeniable and individual.

PARASITE & MY FAIR LADY
My fair lady and parasite
Recently, I watched two very different films and although not my intention at the outset I noticed a number of striking similarities and instructive differences that merit further exploration.
The films were Lerner and Loewe s 1964 musical My Fair Lady (based on Shaw s 1913 play Pygmalion) and Bong Joon Ho s 2019 thriller Parasite.
At first glance, these two films make an odd and counterintuitive pairing but closer inspection reveals many shared characteristics around perennially relevant themes, namely class, language, fate, and sex.
Turning first to class, the most obvious parallel between the two films is their treatment of high society versus low and the various means by which these distinctions/barriers are reinforced and transgressed.
In My Fair Lady, we have the ill-bred Eliza Doolittle, a flower girl in the slums of Covent Garden, and her good-for-nothing father, a dustman with a penchant for drink and little else. Then, across town, we have the cantankerous and highly regarded Professor Higgins, a philologist, linguist, and incorrigible misogynist with upper-class connections that he both loathes and takes for granted. 
While largely destitute, the Covent community is portrayed sympathetically, even idyllically, as a site of joviality, bonhomie, and good-natured ignorance. Eliza and her father are happy in their way. Then comes the Professor, a colonizing presence, who along with his chum Pickering set out on a lighthearted project of civilizing the feral Eliza, and elevating her to socioeconomic status through (and this will become important later on) language, diction, and elocution.
The project proves a success although, as Higgins learns, not entirely for the reasons he initially supposed. His introduction of a common flower girl to the upper crust of Edwardian London rests not on his ability to tame the wild Cockney tongue, but rather on Eliza s ability to adapt her lingual evolution being merely a manifestation of the internal strength both of her class and (it is implied) of her sex.
Similarly, Parasite centers on the socioeconomic tensions between lower class and upper, and attempts to navigate the two; however, in this gritty thriller, the protagonist is not a single character but a family, the Kims, who eke out a living folding pizza boxes and siphoning off their neighbors Wi-Fi (i.e. hijacking the network significant).
However, after finding themselves the recipient of a scholar stone and a tutoring opportunity with the wealthy Parks family the Kims  fortune begins to change as they find it remarkably easy to manipulate the  host  family and gradually infiltrate all of the principal positions the household staff in  parasitic  fashion.
As in My Fair Lady, there is clear delineation between the classes, both in terms of their external circumstance and their inner characteristics, but here in Parasite, we find no idealism, no idyllic portrayals of the underprivileged as a hardy, rollicking, vital bunch. Quite the contrary, poverty is conveyed in all of its squalor, its discomfort, its desperation. There are no spontaneous outbursts of song, no quaint bouquets to mask the stench.
Much like Eliza s semi-subterranean dwelling which we glimpse in an early scene, the Kims live in a tenement basement, and as we later find out the former housekeeper s husband has been kept in a hidden bunker for the past four years, dodging loan sharks.
The contrast of high and low, chthonic and solar, ascent and descent, is perhaps more readily apparent in Parasite, but can also be found in My Fair Lady as well, as stairways are mounted and descended in the lively dance of social (im)mobility that the film explores.
Where Eliza lightly dances, the Kims must scrabble and brawl. Both face obstacles and barriers to entry, but the nature of those barriers and the means by which they are overcome (or not) vary dramatically.
First off, in My Fair Lady, a means of upward access is benevolently (read: condescendingly) offered from above on a mercurial whim whereas the Kims must construct their own means of ascent through duplicity, violence, and coordinated effort.
That s not to say, however, that there is not an element of duplicity in My Fair Lady, for there is. In fact, the attempt to obscure or mask Eliza s origins and  pass her off  as the titular fair lady is in many respects the core conceit of the film. Can the leopard change its spots or, in this case, its voice? This is the primary barrier to entry that Shaw concerned himself with in the play on which the film is based  language. Determining the role that language plays in reinforcing and reifying social hierarchies is Higgin s life s work, laboring assiduously under the theory that the thin veil of accent, articulation, and presentation is the only thing separating the  guttersnipe  from the lady. 
He sets out to prove this with Eliza and partially succeeds, but fails insofar as his initial theory was predicated on the notion of language as merely lingual, a series of sounds uttered in such and such a way and not an expression of something fundamental, something extrasensory.
This is also tied up intimately with sex and sexual difference, yet another barrier that the film interrogates, and one that Higgins himself must overcome through, yes, language, but also deed ( Show Me ). When, in the final scene, he plays the recording of Eliza s voice in all its vulgar glory, he is not simply hearing the mispronunciation and crass elisions, he is hearing the woman herself, seeing her face, feeling the presence to which he s grown so gradually and imperceptibly accustomed.
In this sense, My Fair Lady seems to suggest that language is both an obstruction and the means by which said obstruction may be cleared not language as sound, but language as love a trite observation, but a true(?) one.
Language is also a employed as a mechanism of upward mobility in Parasite (e.g. the Kim daughter using a cursory Google search to quickly internalize and regurgitate pseudo-psychological mumbo jumbo that lends her an air of legitimacy and allows her to secure the position as art therapist for the troubled Park boy); however, attempts at linguistic gap-bridging break down quickly in the face of the disastrous exigences that soon arise, giving way instead to the universal language of violence (e.g. the harrowing climax in which the Kim girl and Mr Park are murdered during a birthday party).
Ultimately, the primary barrier that holds the Kim family back is not that of sound, but that of smell. From the opening shot of socks drying in the basement window to the closing shot of the same, Parasite is full of visual and diegetic touches that invoke the olfactory sense, usually toward revulsion.
Much is made of the  line  that separates the Park family from its staff, and Mr. Park in particular is insistent on this line not being crossed. While complimentary of Mr. Kim s skill as a driver/chauffeur and his ability to navigate the implicit boundaries without any verbal transgression, he complains that his peculiar odor (like radishes or  old rags that have been boiled ) does in fact cross the line. His wife later stops her nose and cracks the window. Even the Park s son comments upon the scent, noting the similarity of Mr. Park and Mrs. Park s miasma, threatening to undo their whole operation. 
During the climactic party scene, it is Mr Park s visible revulsion at the smell of his former housekeeper s husband who has unbeknownst to him been a loyal resident of the house for nigh on half a decade, that Mr. Kim snaps and plunges the knife into his host s back, a suicidal act given that he himself is a parasitic member of the  biome of affluence. 
Smell and sound both are sensory points of slippage in this pair of films that beckon toward class difference as the internalization of superficial externalities externalities that manifest as codified bodies of social practice.
In My Fair Lady, there are strict codes of conduct that Eliza must learn to abide by before she can be ushered in as a member of the privileged elite. In Parasite,  code  instantiates not simply as behavioral expectations on the horizonal plane but also as a system of vertical communication, exchanged in the form of dots and dashes. The flickering lights that the housekeeper s former husband uses to express his unacknowledged gratitude to Mr. Park is also, ironically, the same mechanism that Mr. Kim uses to make contact with his son after taking refuge in the very same bunker, a way of collapsing and signaling across distance. 
Codes, rules, and roles are bound up in one another across both films, but particularly in My Fair Lady, where Eliza must master a set of seemingly arbitrary guidelines in order to  play the role  convincingly. Each member of the Kim family also assumes at various points in the film an identity that is not their own with an eye toward  inhabitation  of and resettlement within the Parks household (which, suggestively, was designed by a famous architect, yet another gesture toward artifice). 
The idea of resettlement or forced relocation is particularly compelling in light of the young Park s boy obsession with Native American culture. Both films investigate to various degrees the relations between colonizer and colonized, with Eliza undergoing forced reeducation under the hegemonic tutelage of Professor Higgins, and the Parks (particularly Mr. Park) being forced to engage in playfully appropriative reenactments of violence perpetrated by and against the native, an implicit stand in for the subjugated masses on whose backs the privileged few stand.
We ve already discussed the significance of ascent and descent within these socioeconomic frames of reference, but there is also an element of forward and backward motion i.e. the transportational means by which class is reinforced and appropriate distances maintained.
When Eliza first makes her way to Professor Higgins  abode for an unannounced parlay, she does so by taxi, a luxurious mode of travel that causes considerable wonder among her fellow lowlifes back in Covent Gardens. After her transformation into lady, she returns in the same mode of conveyance. In Parasite, also, vehicular transportation functions as a symbol for power relations operating  beneath the hood  of cosmetic decorum. Mr Kim may be at the wheel, but he does not dictate where the car goes. His agency is derivative of and subordinate to the passenger s will.
Later as he and his children make their late-night escape from the Park residence, having just avoided detection after their hosts return early from a thwarted camping trip, they must make their way home barefoot in the rain.
Upon arriving, the discover that their basement dwelling has been flooded with sewage water, once again evoking the sense of olfactory disgust and germophobic privilege of the viewing audience. The toilet becomes a site of reverse excretion, a spewing upward, a seeping out. This unsanitary deluge is foreshadowed by the drunk man urinating outside the Kim s home near the beginning of the film, positioning the human as a product of its own waste, emitting a foul stench that must be cleansed through psychosocial ablution (see, for contrast, the comical bath scene in Lady).
Of course for every output (feces, urine, the unhygienic Mass) there is input (nutritive substance, genetic material). Parasite also deals heavily with consumption and ingestion both in the material and metaphysical sense.
Food figures heavily in several key scenes, not least of which is the Kims  brief period of reckless indulgence/imbibery in the Park s absence. Then there is the notable detail of the bunker s entranceway, hidden as it is behind a well-stocked pantry shelf a bounty inaccessible to the prisoners of poverty below. Finally, there is the birthday cake, a treat which the young Park boy is illicitly enjoying under cover of darkness when he encounters the ghost or apparition that, in point of fact, is merely the former housekeeper s husband ascending from the bunker for the same purpose.
Traumatized by this incident, the eccentric and coddled Park boy is thought to have some sort of early onset schizophrenia, a fear that the Kim girl preys upon with her speculative diagnoses as the boy s pseudo-therapist.
In My Fair Lady, food functions less as a necessity withheld and more as an enticement proffered. Using her hunger against her (specifically her penchant for treats, an infantilizing detail that could perhaps offer parallels to the bunker dweller s regressive bottle-feeding and banana-eating), Professor Higgins dangles the prospect of limitless chocolates before Eliza as an incentive to serve as experimental subject in his wager, later appealing to this exploitive transaction as evidence of good treatment.
Later on, as her basic needs are met, the offer of chocolate no longer serves as sufficient enticement, having been made aware of higher needs, emotional needs, as of yet unfulfilled.
It is in this dimension, the emotional dimension, that she becomes the familiar ghost that haunts Professor Higgins  austere and sexless life, leaving with her feigned departure an emptiness that no gramophone recording or scholarly pastime could fill.
Thus we see in both films Hunger and Apparition acting as dual manifestations of basic human impulses across carnal and metaphysical axes.
While transgression of this axis does occur, it s not through lack of enforcement. In Parasite, enforcement comes both from within (the former housekeeper s video evidence of the Kims deceit) and without (the investigative and legal measures taken to apprehend and penalize the Kims for their crimes).
In Lady, Zoltan Karpathy plays the prosecutorial part, in an attempt to wrest Eliza of her mask and expose her as the poseur he suspects her to be. In this sense, he functions as a kind of exterminator, intent on ridding old money of its parasitic hangers-on (one of which, ironically, he himself is). Beyond the name, Joon-Ho's film gestures toward this parasitic relationship in many ways, most notably in one of its early scenes, where the windows are left cracked as an insect-repelling spray truck passes in hope of ridding the basement dwelling of its stink bug infestation (again, smell).
In both cases, the protagonists  ability or failure to escape extermination, detection and/or incrimination is reliant upon two key factors: fortune/luck and rhetorical/choreographic skill.
Turning first to fortune, we ve already mentioned the symbolic significance of the scholar s stone as a supposed portent of fortune; ironically, it is this very stone which the former housekeeper s husband uses as a blunt instrument to bash the Kim boy s head in. 
Significantly, the boy s injury at the hands of the crazed basement dweller (and the family s initial encounter with the former housekeeper and her husband in the bunker, during which they are held hostage by way of extortion) can be credited to a moment of slippage on the dank stairway. The notion of slipping, letting the mask fall away, is as we ve seen present in both films.
In Lady, slippage is also an ever-present reality, but  with a little bit of luck,  a positive outcome is guaranteed and with this as his motto, Eliza s father freeloads his way through life, not a care in the world.
The Kims are also the beneficiaries of many lucky turns during their meteoric rise to success, but ultimately, they are undone by the very blind luck on which they ve grown to depend ( The best plan is not to have one,  says Mr Park to his son during their stay in the crisis shelter, post-flood).
The scholar s stone serves its master, so to speak, and while the Kim s do derive transitory benefits from possession thereof, they are ultimately crushed under the weight of accumulated wealth that the stone represents (stratification, the  upper crust ). The term  scholar s stone  is of interest here, with the academic connotations of the name and its bestowal from the hands of a university student. In Lady as well, it is the well-educated Professor who  gifts  the heavy instrument (here figured as language and the ponderous tome) to undeserving hands Eliza may fling her slippers in retaliation, the Kims may plunge their knives, but what are they against the weight of privilege, wealth, and refinement?
This leads us to the second factor by which upward mobility is determined: rhetorical skill and one s ability to exploit the system s moral elasticity through choreographies of language.
At first, the Kim s prove themselves more than capable of manipulating the refined naivete of the Park family to their own advantage through keen observation, tireless rehearsal, and coordinated execution. They are not bad people, as such, but they feel compelled by circumstance to engage in morally dubious acts, justifying these actions through rationalization (as the Kim boy says after forging a diploma,  I just printed it out early ), appeals to inequity (Mrs. Kim:  I d be nice too if I were rich ) or though outright denial of moral system (in Mr. Kim s words,  None of it fucking matters ).
However, despite their moral acrobatics and choreographies, their failure to account for certain contingencies and eventualities ultimately proved to be their undoing, just as the mysterious workings of sexual relation detonated the Professor s fa ade of invulnerability, revealing a phonetic substrate deeper than that of mere vocality a language of the heart, the lines between the lines.
As a musical, Lady lends itself more readily to the choreographic readings, with elaborate numbers, sets, and dances intended to evoke certain emotions and set certain tones that propel the narrative forward. 
These visual choreographies are reflected in the rhetorical routines that Higgins rehearses in his study as he, quite skillfully, attempts to plug up the holes in the rapidly crumbling dam of emotional detachment and sexual abstainment. Eliza s father also displays considerable aplomb in his ability to manipulate the mores of the time, albeit in largely unscripted manner (again,  with a little bit of luck  it will all work out).
However, even he falls prey to the evils of  middle class morality,  a grim and liminal space wherein the carefree mirth of low-class freeloading and the langour of the upper class privilege are both just out of reach, the only recourse being that of stolid participation in the doleful rituals of normalcy (I.e. the wedding day).
For a brief period, in the wake of his subterranean trauma, the Kim boy experiences a detached lightness, finding himself incapable of any response save laughter when exposed to the horrors of his situation. But eventually he is tugged back into the tumult by the dots and dashes of desperation, making clear his intention to rescue his father from the chthonic torments of guilt and indigence.
So to conclude, in Lady, we are told the difference in class is collapsible through the language of love. In Parasite, there is no such reassurance. Slippage will always occur at the least opportune moment and not even the most well-conducted attempt at coded communication, infiltration, and complicitous critique will change that. Or will it? Parasite 2, coming soon to a theater near you.

PIG
A short tender film that weeps with its viewers. That celebrates life in light of (not in spite of) death. 
The truffle is mammon, the highly sought-after thing. The pig helps us find it. Only in the dying of the pig and the crying of the man does the known become felt. We eat the dying as remembrance, a sacred communing (Pentecost - ooohh I m on fire). Do not barter flesh for flesh, sucking tubes and roaring engines, the crass exchange. The bridges will fall, the lights will blink out, the high places will bebrought down. Seek ye first the pig and all these truffles will be added unto you. 
Note: The power of the pig is not the power of the dog. They both possess an animal keenness, but where the dog devours, the pig empowers. The dog is waiting fangs bared--in the high hills, the pig is rooting in the low wet forest. 
Also, see the piano as a pig-totem, a stand in for memory, a traumatic clinging, a blood sacrifice for the restoration of one s authentic voice (press play)
