THE LIGHTHOUSE
Short
Accidentally watched the lighthouse (2016) instead of the 2019 dafoe vehicle by the same name. apparently both films are inspired by the same real life tragedy that occurred in 1801 at the smalls lighthouse, where one of the keepers died in a freak accident and the other was forced to  keep the light  on his own, losing his sanity in the process. the 2016 film starts strong, with powerful performances from both actors; however, as it goes on it tests the endurance of the viewer as much as the benighted keeper, haunted by the stray arm of his companions dangling corpse,  beckoning him toward judgment. I suppose in this sense the film succeeds, relentless in its attack upon the viewers patience, sanity, dashing much of its symbolic potential upon the rocks of tedium and generic convention.

Long
Chris Crow s The Lighthouse (2016) is a psychological thriller based on the true story of two lighthouse keepers, Thomas Griffths and Thomas Howell, the former of whom dies in a freak accident while tending the Smalls Lighthouse during a weeks-long gale, leaving his guilt-wracked companion to keep the light alone. 
Rooted firmly in a sense of time and place (i.e. a remote and barren island off the coast of Wales in early-nineteenth century), the film sets out a grim and harrowing tale from the annals of maritime tragedy, with powerful performances from its two main actors Mark Lewis Jones as the salty, laconic Griffiths who worships no god but the sea, and Michael Jibson as the hapless, troubled Howell, whose negligence at his former post cost the lives of six sailors. 
Although the film lays itself out in commendably straightforward fashion, with little thematic or artistic pretension, there are moments of genuine brilliance, particularly in the first act as these two characters are forced to interact within the claustrophobic confines of the lighthouse quarters, coming to terms with the fact that they will likely die there as the storm sets in and grows in unabated fury, precluding the possibility of relief. Haunted by the strung-up corpse of the man he has not murdered (but for whose death he is sure to be blamed, as he was for the ill-fated 6), Howell begins a descent into madness that offers interesting (albeit poorly explored) metaphorical possibilities in  light  of the tower s precipitous ascent, the onslaught of the waves (unconscious depths, psychic tumult), the apparitional fogs, and the isolation of the film s setting.
And here is where a competent if slightly low-budget picture becomes an exercise in sheer endurance. I suppose, in this sense, the film could be said to succeed, launching as relentless an attack upon the viewer s sanity as that of the poor tormented Howell, but in the process, regrettably, it also dashes much of its symbolic potential upon the rocks of tedium and generic convention.
While Dave Egger s The Lighthouse (2019) bears on its surface a striking resemblance to Crow s earlier film of the same time (to an extent that some might call borderline plagiaristic), the two films could not be more different in terms of tone, cinematographic merit, and intended function. True, both films center on a pair of Toms (older and younger) tending to a lighthouse on a remote island, and true, both convey their subsequent descent into madness and the death of the principal keeper during an unrelenting storm, but the manner in which these events unfold and the way in which they are conveyed and framed are distinct, even antithetical.
Crow s Lighthouse is, as mentioned, rooted firmly in historical and geographical specificities, whereas Egger s operates on the higher plane of archetype: a troubled and rebellious Seeker holding forth with the old and mercurial Keeper on a timeless and placeless Island above which rises the Tower of Light, shot and framed in mythic style. 
The technical qualities of both films also bears mentioning here: while Crow s color picture features a grim and muted palette along with relatively conventional camerawork (as befits the harrowing simplicity of the thriller), Egger s allegorical opus boldly declares itself a masterpiece with carefully composed shots, portentous angles, and heavy use of shadow, further heightened by the film s (admittedly stunning) use of black and white.
This all-pervading sense of self-importance and self-awareness extends even and especially to the dialog, a cyclonic admixture of Biblical idiom and nautical jargon that is equal parts Old Testament prophet and Moby Dick, a fact humorously acknowledged in Howard s vitriolic rant against Wake s Neptunian pomposity, (which, come to think of it, might be read as sly pre-anticipation of any criticism against the film s  Captain Ahab horseshit. )
This actually brings me to one of my primary complaints about the film namely, its insistence upon puncturing or deflating the gravitas that it so masterfully cultivates with the occasional vulgarity, scatological reference, or crass insertion. The much-lauded  Hark!" monologue was, for me, one of the most problematic and jarring moments in the film, squandering boat loads of dynamic energy on so petty a thing as a squabble over culinary preference:  Alright, have it your way. I like your cookin . 
Through Crow s Lighthouse may at times bore the viewer,  it cannot be said to disrespect him. There is no fourth wall breaking, no tongues in cheeks, no sidelong winking. It tells the tale straight and in my opinion reasonably well, given the obviously limited budget.
Egger s film, on the other hand, is a well-funded endeavor brimming with talent, both directorial, technical, and thespian. I could go on at length, and I m sure some have, about the film s sound design, psychosexual evocations, mythic allusions, and metaphorical frameworks relative to Crow s (up vs. down, in vs. out) but for the purposes of this comparative review, I feel the need to point out and honestly, to condemn Egger s refusal to permit us the luxury of shock and awe. 
On this point, and this point alone, Crow s film is superior and I stand by that assessment.

WHAT WE DO IN THE SHADOWS
Finished season 3 of What We Do in the Shadows. Not as good as I remember the first season being less of the situational subtlety/absurdity that one expects from a Matt Berry (side?) vehicle (and British alt comedy in general) and more wacky gags of the US mockumentary variety. Suppose that s to be expected for any show that finds a broader audience. 
Anyhow, it got me thinking about vampirism as a metaphor for the human condition, particularly when held against that other mainstay of allegorical supernatural horror, the zombie flick.
Ligotti touched on this in Conspiracy against the Human Race, pointing both to the tradition of vampiric literature and to the notion of zombification as examples of man s split nature neither living nor dead but an unholy combination of the two,  turned  by macabre forces beyond ourcontrol and driven toward the autocannabalistic lust for flesh (the brain, the blood, the  food that talks )
While there are similarities between the zombie and the vampire, there seem to me to be instructive distinctions as well, particularly when considered in light of Jungian thought, i.e. his theory of mass mindedness vs individuation. Yes, both the zombie and the vampire are compelled by dark consumptive desires over which they have no control, but their divergent levels of awareness of this fact (a total lack on the part of brain eaters and an excess on the part of blood suckers) renders the former trite and the latter genuinely tragic. 
While the zombie operates as part of a mindless horde, seeking to consume the grey matter he no longer possesses, the vampire is keenly aware of his plight, and thus spends his days in a state of grim solitude still human by most appearances, but unable to join his fellow man in the sunlight of sociality. He is a creature of the night, pariah, embittered, powerful and sensitive. the zombie on the other hand is just a slavering bundle of decomposition.
Both could be read as shadow selves of a sort, one as nega-ego, the other as pure id, but the vampire I think comes closer to the Jungian mark insofar as he knows himself to be a monster and must grapple eternally with the existential nightmare of his parasitic existence. 
While Shadows makes light (lol) of the standard conventions of vampire media (to diminishingly comedic effect as the characters become flanderized versions of their season 1 selves) it also engages with some of the enduring themes previously mentioned: mortality, self-predation, the uncanny, etc. 
Re: the uncanny, there s Nadja s double in doll form (doll-pperganger?), an inanimate toy possessed by the spirit of its owner, functioning as an ego totem of sorts, an evocation of man as his own puppet, the Pinocchio whose nose grows inward with every lie, slowly piercing his cruelly oversized brain.
There is also Nandor s existential crisis upon learning of the Big Bang Theory and materialist determinism (ie the uncaring universe), his subsequent attempt to sublimate this  knowledge  through delusional participation in the cult of optimism (forceful extraction of the fangs, a futile attempt to extirpate his true nature and thereby salvage meaning from the endless void), and finally his decision to enter a centuries long Super Slumber vis a vis Mainlander s will to die and the antinatalist tradition as a whole. 
Ultimately however, this is a moderately successful tv show so of course our main character decides life is worth living after all, and he giddily sets off to see the world in all of its meaningless variety (eat, prey, love). very depressing. 5.5/10

X-MEN (& LIGOTTI)
Caught the end of X-Men: first class.
Was struck by its treatment of mutation as a thinly veiled metaphor for human consciousness and the strange set of powers with which it endows us, powers that set us irrevocably apart from the natural world, no matter how hard we try to reconcile our differences and subordinate our conscious capacities to its implacable whims. In conspiracy against the human race, Ligotti also uses the vocabulary of mutation and perversion as analogical to man s self-awareness and he too would agree that we are set apart to suffer the weight of difference and dual personhood as both selves and non-selves.  However, with its beatification of Professor X as apostle/martyr of optimism, (in contrast to the grim Nietzschean gospel of Magneto), the film holds onto the possibility of redemptive action, of a new symbiosis between the mutant and the man, the conscious and the unconscious...a softening of the evolutionary toward alleviation of pain and accommodation of difference. Ligotti makes no such allowances and would, along with Zapffe and other pure pessimists of his ilk, decry the film as an admittedly inventive but ultimately insufficient attempt at preserving an unfounded future that we are unlikely to achieve

9 BILLION NAMES OF GOD
i read the 9 billion names of god, titular short story in a collection by arthur clarke. very short and simple, perhaps a bit too simple.. concept seems a bit thin.. some tibetan monks use a supercomputer to generate all possible names of god via brute force permutation...9-vharacter strings in a language of their own devising. upon completion of the task, the world ends. huh? the thematics are fleshed out not at all. gestures lazily at the joint & respective functions of religion, language, and technology and--I suppose--the thanatophilia thay underlies them all. maybe the mechanics of the modern world are simply hastening an inevitable exhaustion/depletion of all semantic possibilities, at which point the symbols cease to signify and the stars blink out one by one, "without fuss" (i liked the ending)

CHARLEY & THE CHOCOLATE FACTORY
Encountered an interesting reading of Charley and the Chocolate Factory as  soteriological story  wherein Wonka functions as God figure and the children as prospective initiates into his Eleusinian mysteries, full participation in which can only be achieved through a harrowing process of descent (i.e. into the heart of the factory as Testing or Proving ground), searching/striving (i.e. undergoing a series of temptations), and finally ascent (up and unto the lofty heights of divine perspective and insight, figured as glass elevator, the speed and maneuverability of which stands in stark contrast to cheap thrill of the purloined fizzy lifting drink). The fates and failings of the various children lends them an allegorical aspect (Gloop as Gluttony, Salt as Pride, Teevee as Sloth, etc.) with only Charley emerging victorious. In this sense, he sits squarely within the classical heroic tradition, a moral exemplar/paragon of virtue whose temperance, endurance, and humble origin renders him the only worthy successor to Wonka's empire of Joy and Play. The golden ticket then is no mere fungible token, monetary/material asset, or social credit, but rather a kind of moral voucher, a  key to the kingdom  which is not of this world but of one beyond, and which consequently only the meek may inherit.

ON RELIGION
Schle & Jung (first speech)
Schleiermacher's first speech from On Religion while a far cry from Jung's crisp analytical prose seems to presage many elements of Jungian thought, most notably, the split nature of man, the primacy of experience, the role of religion as a integrative force, and the necessity of the mediatorial or priest-figure (for Jung, the psychoanalyst, and for Schleiermacher, the "God-inspired person"). I was particularly struck the German theologian's theory of expansion and attraction as the dual forces at work in man (which apparently was a popular concept in the natural philosophy of the day), forming a polar relationship (the "great series") at the far extremities of which lie idealism and hedonism respectively, each tending toward self-isolation and -deprivation in an eternal cycle of (f)utility that fails to reckon with its source. Nature, however, distributes the dual urges in equal measure throughout its parts, compelling all things toward and against the center, the innermost depths, the divinely charged origin point from which springs (or through which is mediated) religious experience in all its singularity and instinctive power, and of which the cold rationalist, utilitarian moralist finds unable (because unwilling [because naturally predisposed toward unwillingness]) to conceive except as a crude instrument or vestige.

Schle & Jung (second speech)
Working thru Schleiermacher s second speech and am continuously struck by the parallels between or preanticipations of Jungian thought, particularly in his love of pairs (theoretical/practical, moral/metaphysical, attractive/expansive, speculation/praxis, individuality/multiplicity) and his positioning of religious intuition as the "necessary and indispensable third" that enables man to reckon with/sublimate his shadow (ie his inborn promethean hubris) into a larger understanding/experience of the divine unity made manifold.

Intuition
According to Schle. as I (probably incorrectly) read him, true religion (i.e. intuition of the infinite from the particular) is necessarily void of content. Or more precisely, it is itself a void through which content flows but cannot linger.
Think of a tube, whose constraints make possible the transfer of fluids from one space to another (expansion/contraction, theory/practice, multiplicity/individuality, morality/metaphysics), a mediating channel through which hydrodynamic "instincts" can become realized.
The function of the conduit is in no way dependent on the nature of the material flowing through it--it matters only that movement takes place unobstructed.
Stop it up, however, at one end or the other--attempt to convert the dynamic conduit to a static container and the contents become stagnant, lifeless, incapable of fulfilling the divine imperative toward motion.
So does the religious instinct become nothing more than "empty mythology" as soon as the immediacy of the particular instance is subordinated to abstract notions of authoritative dogma or theological system. 
Schle. does not seem to be in any way maligning the role that system plays in human life; he is simply pointing out that systematics have no place in religion. it ought not be conceived of as a body of accumulated material (mythical, doctrinal, or other), but rather as the State or Medium by which bodies are moved or made to move through impulse.

Particularity vs. Multiplicity
In thinking about Schle. I m beginning to realize that I may have misunderstood his use of  particularity  vs  multiplicity  as they relate to unity. I initially assumed the two terms were being used synonymously, but on further reflection, I think they are more accurately conceived as constitutive, which is to say the former (the particularity) functions as one of the many instances that make up the latter (the multiplicity) and by reason of its dual nature thereby gestures toward unities both greater and lesser, within and without, micro and macrocosms expanding and contracting in relation to one another

Oldenberg & Schle ("thirdplace")
Sociologist Ray Oldenburg speaks of the importance and regrettable absence of the  third place  in the modern man s life, his  home away from home  that provides a vital zone for socialization and reflection outside of home and work. 
Reading Schleiermacher s assertion of religion as the  indispensable third  that accommodates/supersedes the moralistic impulse on the one hand and the metaphysical on the other, I can t help but think of the sacred site or community as perhaps slotting into Oldenberg s framework, with moral praxis representing home life and philosophical speculation functioning as a kind of  work,  the equilibrium between which (and proper conception of which) can only be achieved through appreciation for & inhabitation of the religious zone (i.e. the seat of inspiration and instinctual participation in the divine unity made manifest/manifold). 
Not sure how or if Schleiermacher would frame religion in terms of Place, whether church or temple or perhaps nature itself (given his Romantic leanings), but it seemed like a connection worth making

PROFESSOR OF DESIRE
Just finished Roth s *Professor of Desire*. A light read. The protagonist is a smart good-looking Jewish kid with a flair for the theatrical, a gift for the rhetorical, and a taste for the carnal. Equal parts libido and cogito, David Kepesh attempts to navigate the treacherous waters of desire and despair--Scylla of mind on the one hand (compulsive self reflection, paralysis by analysis) and Charybdis of flesh on the other (hopeless susceptibility to female charm, hedonic abandonment).
The psychosexual coordinates of David s academic/erotic career can, I think, be charted relative to his major literary influences at each stage: the typewriter/drum (the syncopated allure of showmanship and stagecraft, a la Herbie s flatulent exhibitionism), Dostoevsky (the crime of morose association with  practicing homosexual  Jeselnik, the punishment of Silky s sex withheld) Icelandic verse (the epic saga of Brigitte s lust and Elizabeth s lament [keening/kenning]), Chekhov (the domestic strangulation of his failed marriage to the exotic Helen), erotic verse (the incorrigible Baumgarten and his unflinching vulgarities, welcome distractions from the glum post divorce caesura), and at last Kafke (the sense of futility and doom pervading even David s  happy ending  with sensible, tender young Claire, wife material if ever there was such a thing). Desire: cupid s poison tipped arrow, the sweetness unto death (petit mort)

VACCINE
sickness is an opening up and a coming in, perversely. The well may employ the sick function as Vaccine, or, a re-versioning of the perverse (not a cancellation of the perverse but a multiplication of it). 
When power wields the means of vaccination, then should the well fear. For that which is vaccinated against may be the very thing the Body needs most. What is learned is this: that to be well is to have been sick. 
When the body rejects the foreign substance, it rejects a becoming, for good or ill. When other than the self holds sway over this becoming, then should the self fear. 
But does not the World hold sway over the self, to introduce or withhold? No. For world demonstrably is self and self world. Seizure of the world-self and containment of its workings are figured as: the Tube or Needle, forcefully inserted.
What to do, we who are sufferers of the real and imagined? Find joy in the foreign body as in the body of the self, forsaking fear of fear: intubated Other.
The victim, friend, is no one and the death toll but One.

HAPPINESS
The modern quest for happiness is a Sisyphean exercise in futility. How can you achieve a state that you cannot define? 
You reply,  well I know happiness when I feel it. 
And under what conditions are those feelings produced? You may point to the hierarchy of needs: physiological, safety, belongingness, esteem, self-actualization. 
This, we are told, is where happiness resides in the fulfillment of our various needs. 
Very well. Let s examine fulfillment. To  fill  a thing implies that it was once empty. Given enough time, it will be empty again. 
To be  happy  then, is to be actively, dynamically fulfilling needs as they arise.
The cycle continues; there seems to be no point of ultimate achievement, at least, not on our plane of timebound, embodied existence. 
Given the cyclical relational interplay that exists between fulfillment and depletion (yin and yang), how can we hope to Proceed or Advance in any meaningful way? At what point in this cycle is happiness said to be attained and/or lost? What are the units, the system of measurement? How many needs must go unmet before one can be designated unhappy or vice versa? Where is the  fill line ?
You reply,  well you can t quantify a feeling in that way it s more subjective.  
This, of course, brings us back to the initial objection namely, that you cannot achieve that which you cannot define. 
Of course, defining human needs at the basic level is simple enough: food, shelter, clothing, etc. These needs are universal and constant across age, time, and culture.
But at the higher levels of belonging and actualization, we find ourselves unable to articulate or even conceptualize our needs caught in a muddle of often contradictory expectations, presuppositions, (pre)conceptions, and societal norms.
To make sense of it all, modern man relies largely on media, branding, and consumptive identity.  I m a [redneck, intellectual, activist]; thus, for me happiness/fulfillment looks like [cold beer/hot babes, cocktail parties/repartee, punk aesthetics/performative acts of social justice].  
The problem, of course, is that these prepackaged identities and their respective trappings and accoutrement do not always accommodate shifts in disposition or the vicissitudes of circumstance. What happens when you are no longer able to enact the identity you ve chosen or maintain the lifestyle that you ve come to associate with happiness?
Poor health, old age, bad luck, mistreatment at the hands of others there are any number of disruptive factors that can (and ultimately will) wrest the illusion of control from our grasp, rendering us existentially adrift.  
No matter what tack we take, no matter what technologies or philosophies we employ in our endeavors to guard against contingencies, the great boulder called Happiness will always slip from our grasp at the last moment, leaving us to wonder, to curse, to crave.  Is it something I did? Something I said? Is it providence, karma, dumb random chance? Why am I not happy despite it all? 
You are not happy, oh modern man, because you do not know what happiness is. 
You do not know what happiness is, oh modern man, because cannot identify your deepest need.
You cannot identify your deepest need, oh modern man, because and now at last we come to it you never needed anything at all. Or, put another way, the only thing you need is no-thing. This, in turn, is the key to every-thing.
Camus said,  One must imagine Sisyphus happy.  But that isn t quite right. The word, as we have seen, is too tired, too bankrupt, too freighted with imprecise connotations. Let us, instead, imagine Sisyphus Content. Accepting of.
This is what I propose Contentment and Acceptance, not happiness, as the state toward which one should allow himself to strive.
And how does one grant the allowance to strive? It is (as with everything [i.e. nothing]) a paradoxical co-accommodation, best conceived of as a triangulation: Passivity, Simplicity, Linearity. 
At the center of these three vertices lies the wellspring, the Is-ness, Was-ness, Yet to Be-ness, that runs through all things at all points always circumscribed and circumscribing, angular and ovular.
 So it is. So I am.  Acceptance, the manifold threefold. Elucidate:
?	Passivity 
?	Simplicity
?	Linearity

ANIMAL THEOLOGY
Chapter 5 
Linzey uses Anne Rice s Interview with a Vampire as an entry point into metaphorical exploration of the parasitical relationship that exists between mankind and the natural world. Objectors might appeal to the beauty of nature and natural law as a sufficient basis for moral action an ecological theology if you will. However, Linzey counters that there exists a critical distinction between the Creator and His creation. To make inferences about the divine nature of the former from the fallen nature of the latter is to obviate the need for redemptive action, and thereby to reinforce the brutality that pervades  natural  cycles of consumption and vampiric feeding, pitting the lion against the lamb in direct contradiction of the Isianic vision. In other words, a rehabilitation of natural law and the state of nature is necessary by way of a new trans-natural moral imperative that entails an active striving against our fallen tendencies. Like Rice's Louis, we must grapple with the deep-seated sense that something is not right, despite the insistence of the Lestats of the world that the blood of the weaker is rightfully ours for the taking. We modern westerners are particularly without excuse given the fact that there are viable alternatives available to us (vegetarianism, veganism, etc). Simply put, Christ as eschatological figure and not simply as first-century Palestintian man represents a possibility of global, even universal transformation of the parasitic paradigm. To simply resign ourselves to our fallenness is unacceptable.
Chapter 4 
With the publication of Gutierrez s A Theology of Liberation (1971), there was a widespread shift toward liberational modes of thought in theological discourse. Although this emergent school acknowledges the universal scope of Christ s redemptive work, its focus lies primarily upon the human being as the subject of liberation. Linzey argues that this reinforcement of orthodox humanocentrism is in direct opposition to the katabasis (sacrificial descent) that Christ displayed. A return to a sound Christology* demands an expansion of the liberational mindset to include the non-human realm. Linzey insists upon this point, despite objections that such a shift actually reinforces humanocentrism (by implying that that only humans can affect liberation for animals), inappropriately compares the plight of non-humans to humans (distinction without difference), and has absurdly far-reaching implications (does vegetable life deserve the same level of consideration? What about minerals? yes, with qualifications).
*Christ as 1. co-creator (the logos that undergirds reality), 2. god-incarnate (logos made flesh), 3. new covenant (the Noahic promise), 4. reconciler of ALL things (not just humans), and 5. moral exemplar (displaying a spirit of compassion for and submission to the weak)
Chapter 3 
Linzey refutes the orthodox notion of divine impassability (God as eternally unsuffering), arguing that the incarnational priesthood of Christ necessitates suffering as a redemptive catalyst for transformation of the fallen world through sacrificial acts, not the sacrificing of beast on altars of stone as in the OT but of one s own  flesh  (i.e. the conveniences that our privileged place in nature affords us) on the altar of consciousness, modelling Christ s compassion for and suffering on behalf of the world, even its least inhabitants. Though this framing of the divine nature borders on the heretical (see Patripassianism) Linzey claims, convincingly, that a moral or mystical theology of animals and our relationship to them is an unavoidable corollary of the benevolence revealed plainly in the god-man. We are thus called to become priests unto the world, to reenact Christ s sacrifice toward the weaker, not because they deserve it but precisely because they do not.
Chapter 1
Pr cis: we ought to have reverence toward all forms of life, human or non-human, insofar as they are expressions of divine grace and creative outpouring. This sense of reverence, rooted in love for the Creator, must necessarily extend outward toward His Creation as we fulfill our responsibilities not only toward one another as bearers of the imago dei, but toward all things which likewise bear the Maker s stamp, imbuing them with certain inalienable  rights,  rights that derive not from any attribute of the thing per se (e.g. sentience or rationality), but rather the divine origin of those attributes, infringement upon which is a boldfaced betrayal of the spirit of Christ s self-emptying on our behalf.

THE PIANO
A dark, melancholic film, so pregnant with meaning you nearly see it crowning. Metaphor sits heavily upon the narrative, squashes it flat to the dimension of fable or macabre folklore. I.e., the details of the protagonist s situation and the character of the protagonist herself matter far less than the environment in which she finds herself, the objects she interacts with, and the archetypes she embodies/encounters/is beset by.
This is intentional, no doubt, as suggested by the occurrence of a local shadow play, in which significantly Bluebeard brandishes a comically large key, before murdering his wife in pantomime (and nearly getting murdered himself in real life by the  savages  [the audience?]). 
By playing with shadows in this way, the film positions itself as commentary on artifice, the ways that we reenact past traumas and project them on our interior canvases or staff lines (the  silent  table) a muting, a reduction, a flattening. 
This is what, I think, the piano stands in as an artifact of pain and loss that the protagonist must jettison before she is to find her true voice. 
The chopping off of fingers is also significant in this regard (and also hearkens back to the shadowplay from before, in preparation for which the drama/trauma/thaumaturgist makes as if to attack a hesitant participant s hand with a prop axe), insofar as it signifies the violent act of lessening that must occur in order to make real that which has been long sublimated. 
You will not emerge from the swamp without leaving some piece of you behind, some appendage or baggage, but in so doing by enduring the great trial, cutting loose the weights of the past you regain that part of yourself which the thing merely symbolized.
It s transactional in this sense sex for love, the body for the soul, the ivory keys for the keys to the heart. (Recall: Ada s forbidden lover later, as they are rowing away from the island and Ada suddenly expresses her desire to cast the piano overboard, says  but I have the key, we can fix it  [or something to that effect].)
The piano stood in for Voice, but it was not Voice itself. The object must sink that the Subject may swim or, perhaps, drown trying, lost in voiceless depths.

POSTHOLE*
[abstract: getting beyond the tired and grim "apparatus" of postmodern theory and its machinc body of metaphors. what about a new terminological/analogical frame, rooted in the natural world, unencumbered by anthropocentrism, aired out]
postmodern theory relies on a set of abstracted, unnatural metaphors and terms that destabilize our understanding of the world and our ability to engage with it in natural ways.
I propose a new frame, a new vocabulary for theory that is rooted in the natural world, the fundamental material of all things, the dirt beneath us and the sky above. then I propose that this new frame be applied to postmodern theorists, both individually and in their entirety, to see what can be meaningfully translated to the new natural theory and what fails to hold.
I believe this is necessary if we are to heal the fracture, to again bring ourselves into proper relation with the world around us. It is not regression that I propose, but a reclamation of our birthright, a maturation, an advance beyond this adolescent stage of paranoia, doubt, and misgiving--the age of smoke and mirrors and obfuscation--in which we have too long languished.
so what are these new metaphors? of what is this new "apparatus" composed? it is composed of the thing around us: earth, water, fire, sky. Green things and brown things and red things and blue things, with a great spirit that binds them.
Aristotle and the greeks will be our guide. They will be our virgil through postmodern hells and they will usher us into heaven, a place that we already know and exist within but do not actually inhabit out of suspicion that it is not real. We must make this house our home again and natural theory will help us do it.
(it is worth saying here that I am not laboring under the delusion of being some great innovator. Many have noted the need to get beyond postmodern theory and many attempts have been made to accomplish, or at least the describe/prescribe the steps to undertake, the great task. Metamodernism, post-postmodernism, hypermodernism--these are some of the names proposed for the project. I think they all fail, terminologically and fundamentally, to get at the "root" of the problem. which is to say that they operate toward and against a perverse, distorted, and self-undermining baseline. As such, they relegate themselves to failure from the outset.)
-Green things - plant life, vegetation (insects)
-Brown things - earth and stone and rock (mammal, man)
-Red things - fire, destruction, energy
-Blue things - water (animal: fish and amphibian)
-Spirit - the wind, the breath of god, that gives rise to animal in each of the sphere (man)

the rain came down in sudden sheets
I passed perilously close to curbs
rolling through residential streets
that brim and darkly surge

toward drains and grates
a guttural placation
my car is full of ghosts
Writing their names, dates
in the condensation

Currents run the length of it 
crackling overhead
Rubber burning at the brink of it
Time's watershed

Where the ions in your fingertips
arc unto death
And that hot electric smell is
On your breath

And headlights sweep conical around the bend
And wiper blades smear streetlights, warm and Gaussian
and red

A wave a thousand stories tall 
of static
Rolling, rolling through the grid
Breaks-- slow, emphatic--
Upon the fluttering lids

Of two great eyes
Tuned like dials
To emergency stations

They cannot hear my dark placations 
Those smiling ghosts that fill my car and cloud my gaze
I'll join them one of these old nights
Or one of these howling days

Text: Ligotti frames his exquisitely morose series of essays on antinatalism with the provocative (and unabashedly materialist) assessment of man as a  self-conscious nothing,  a pessimistic assertion which most would reject out of hand as morbid nonsense, running as it does counter to the near-universally held sense of optimism about our nature and future, as promulgated through the hackneyed maxims and trite platitudes that are so often trotted out in daily life, usually in the form of a neat, smug paradox (see Chesterton) intended to safeguard the illusory tranquility of one s selfhood against the barrages of logic and circumstance; however, through the very desperation with which these tortured epigrams are constructed and pronounced, the optimist shows his hand, betraying his own self as the true paradox in question, a self that is not a self at all, but rather an uncanny puppet which broken free of its strings can be neither fully toy nor fully boy only some uncanny conjoining of the two, an abomination of nightmarish aspect whose existence can be only loosely conceived of in works of supernatural horror but never definitively demonstrated, and certainly never widely accepted as fact, since in so doing, we would be forced to encounter the face in the mirror and find ourselves hideously lacking in one sense and grotesquely endowed in the other.

SOUL RETREIVAL
In soul retrieval, professional shaman/quack Villoldo proposes a four chambered model of the psyche as primordial Eden (chamber of wounds, of contracts, of grace, of treasure). These chambers map astonishingly well to the four-phrase mantra of Ho Opononpono: I am sorry (acknowledgement of mutual wounding), please forgive me (contractual erasure of wrongdoing), thank you (granting of grace), I love you (the largesse, the mother lode/lobe, the Treasure)

UNDISCOVERED SELF
Chapter 1   the plight of the individual in modern society
The plight of the man in modern society is active and invisible obstruction/suppression of self-knowledge (i.e., engagement in/with the irregularities of unmediated, individual experiences of the real) in favor of the massified ego-consciousness of the quasi-animate state that enforces statistical average through scientific rationality  
Chapter 2 - religion as the counter-balance to mass-mindedness
Distinguishing true religion from mere creed, Jung here points out the way in which the dictator states (and the credal religious institutions which subvert them in their insistence on credal conformity) usurps the God function, asserting the primacy of the statistical/scientific mode to the exclusion of any absolute reference point beyond the material/communal, thereby not only robbing man of his rights but also of his instinctive impulse toward the divine and the individuated experiences arising therefrom.
Chapter 3   the position of the west on the question of religion
In this chapter, Jung notes a  fatal parallelism  between the sterile moralities of the western Church-State and the collectivist materialism of the eastern Marxist dictatorships, insofar as both attempt to enforce a set of institutionally sanctioned belief and ideals, which in the case of the West represent a sad reduction/devaluation the riches of our inherited Judeo-Christian tradition with its emphasis upon the sanctity and dignity of the individual life. 
Chapter 4   man s understanding of himself
Positioning consciousness as that fundamental enigma, the precondition of being, which through constant friction against the body of unconscious memory and instinct generates dual and opposite impulses, Jung asserts that a balance must be struck between understanding of the subjectivities and knowledge of man s condition as a whole, in order that the individual man might reclaim the deep symbols of his deep past and integrate them in new and personalized ways, rather than sublimating them through mindless participation in the collective.
Chapter 5   philosophical and psychological approaches to life
In order to heal the disorder of the day (i.e., resolve the neurotic fractures that plague our modern consciousness), Jung asserts that we must undertake a systematic reinvigoration and reexamination of the life-giving symbols and primordial images that we ve inherited (conduits of dynamism and instinctive energies), taking care that we do not lose our shadow selves in collective ideals of statism and fetishization of the abstract Word.
Chapters 6 and 7   self-knowledge and the meaning of self-knowledge
In these final chapters, Jung further interrogates the obstacles which block man s achievement of self-knowledge (i.e. state-driven depotentiation/agglomeration of man as social unit and the isolation of his psychic energies), the means by which he may overcome these obstacles (i.e. personal acknowledgement of the dual nature of man, his shadow self, and the dynamic power of the psychological factor individual numinous encounter), and the importance of doing so (i.e. seizing this historical moment [Kairos   right time] to metamorphosize our tormented psyches, to deepen and broaden the consciousness, to unblock the dynamic flows of primitive  mana  that we might find the happiness and equability that only an individual self, never a state or collective body, can enjoy)
Further reflections
Ligotti and Jung both remark on the strange enigma of consciousness; however, for Ligotti a monistic materialist this puzzle cannot be addressed in any meaningful way as there is no one actually there to solve it (and thus nothing to solve), only agglomerations of matter which, through an unfortunate series of evolutionary/involutionary blunders, fancy themselves souls.
Jung, on the other hand, a committed dualist, is not so quick to dismiss parapsychological phenomena, instead maintaining the distinct possibility that there are psychic realities which supersede their physical manifestations.
In short, where Ligotti sees an illusion or delusion to be annihilated (objective scientific knowledge over subjective understanding), Jung sees a mythic adventure to be undertaken (individual irregularities brought to bear upon the statistical collective).

I come time and again to this point, never beyond it: to fight or surrender. To fight is to pick a side, to stake yourself on a particularity. To surrender is to lay down the notion of sides, to resign yourself to the All-ness.

When I pick a side, I look around and see people dying, and quickly lose whatever taste for the fight I imagined I might have--return to neutral zone, crestfallen and humbled. 

When I surrender, I look around and see people living, and quickly lose whatever capacity for _detachment_ I imagined I might have--return to neutral zone, hot and lusty.

And when I attempt synthesis, that is to engage both impulses simultaneously in some way, to bloody the white flag or dress the red wound, to actively withdraw or to passively engage, I look around and cease to see people at all, just formless smears of half-ideated motion, a kind of mania or derealization--return to neutral zone, dazed and brimming. 

I need to be taken prisoner, conscripted, or else killed. But I cannot be left to my own devices much longer.

as a child, feeling safe was the sublime joy. a rainy day, the smell of something baking, the murmur of a television. Or the sun low in the sky on a drive back from some event or other, buckled up forehead against the window. or your mother putting her hand and to your head, "you're warm." or your father: "we'll figure it out."

what is comparable now, as an adult, grown beyond illusions of domestic safety, of the world as a kind and ordered place? a father God, a mother spirit? a home beyond the clouds? amplified projections of those early sense memories? likely.

but what of the troubled child, who has no such memory bed to rest upon, no loam of parental care from which to grow a spiritual sense? what of them? does not their plight cast doubt on the accidental nature of our optimisms, dependent as they are on experiences we just as easily may never have had?

undoubtedly. whether these doubts are well-founded is another question. institution says no. the zealous practitioner says no. all of our societal buttressing says, desperately, no, no--there is safety in the city, in the nation, in the bosom of progress. 

and yet by the very nature of these protestations against doubt--insistent, plaintive--Man betrays himself, reveals himself a child still, an aging vessel for the flame of nascence, reenactor of the fond recollection.

to those who lack the common ground, the conforming spirit, the stomach for such indulgent shadowplay in the light of some distant happiness--whether real or imagined, future or past--there is no alternative but a new worship, a black flame, a void instead of a God. 

to embrace the existential threat as one would a mother, to find safety in the absoluteness of death: this becomes the exile's creed. so does the occult cyst form below and beyond the town center. and so does the child become its own twin, one evil and one good, each orphaned from itself, lost and toddling about, weary as time, with no one to put him to bed.

Text:
the far off woman
skirt coyly swishing
unattainable, a tan thumbnail of a vision
a moving grove of things soft and hidden, just beyond, 
turning the corner now in miniature 
a hundred paces ahead on an afternoon walk
too many to close space
not so many that I cannot make out the
insouciant gait, the swinging arms
that suggest beauty
of an internal kind
and-- I turn the corner now, heat quivers, noting again the sensuous shifting of haunches
the glimmer of sweat
the play of flesh and muscle
discernable even so far away as
one hundred paces--
of the external kind too

Novelists are primarily driven by the sociological impulse; they are interested in human lives at scale.
Poets are phenomenologists; it is the singular experience that preoccupies them, the texture of the felt, the seen, the heard.
Short story writers are spiritualists; theirs is the realm of symbol and vision and parable.

Whichever impulse holds the most sway in a writer dictates the form he will excel at most.

I heard 
the mother's reville chirping
  from my nested-in eaves
to rouse her eggy young
 out the gnawed through buttonhole
into bright June

threaded along deftly
on a blue strung needle
she sewed to the sky around them 
a breastpocket
of eagerness and care
"see! see! the world!"

ungainly, they fluttered forth 
on downward courses
new to the pull
of wind 
and grass 
and the glinting water's
surface

that gives and swallows,
a chlorinated lethe

it was unlucky variance for one
that took its errant flight path 
to the hot white pool deck 
ill fated

and 
from my porch I watched it,
quizzical,
hop lightly in
(no diving!)
and learn the only thing 
it ever would

about thrust and drag and lift
which it couldnt get 
with its wet wings 
and soft bones

drawing pitiful cursive
on the deep end substrate
lacework ripples
on that lazy blue square 
that no one saw but me
and the recourseless mother
"see! see!"

dashing down,
i hurdled the locked gate
(no running!)
and scooped it up 

too late, 
filmy-eyed
death-spraddled
beak agape and dribbling

it vacated its bowels in my hands
and stiffened

later, inside
hands washed
I chanced to see the mother on the railing
looking in at me

abridges continents

somewhere just beyond the residential zone;

which creak out promises:
ancient, technical,

	patched and clanging, of more-than-human size
	emitting a peculiar song that vibrates in the eyes

he's eating berries in the bushes

to come, to come
and touch his metal carapace
and read the lines,
the brailled and dotted surfaces
the slant rhymes
that groove his chiptune lexicon
reroute the crucifix

a way across or under it

while jesuits
thumb doggedly through codices, manuals--
pages full of broken script
homiletic, illegible
to the insensate, heavy-lipped,
heavy-lidded........

awake! the hissing, writhing nests
long-dormant in the ditches
are out to turn your sons to clowns
daughters into witches!

[autophagic <-> classicized, inert

and saw the beckoning hand reach from shelf

astride an old, old earth]

with sigils drawn in silicon
devices ceremonial
the great snake eats the sun, 
	eats its tail
livewire cannibal

circuit complete
Sam bounds
winking sidelong in peripheries

but his song persists
at ever-higher frequencies

bodhis sat vascillating 
on the stoop
thinking of famine, war 
and croup

these things were not good
they were seemingly bad
he belched and smelled
the meal he just had

a parade was ongoing
projectile vomit
and beads 
and jasmine was growing;
a bum was relieving
himself on its leaves

there were fat men wearing pirate hats
and fat women who appeared to be liking that
and children in strollers
and jackbooted patrollers
and bells and bell tollers
and all the kings horses and all of his men

all of whom bodhis roundly condemned 
there from his place on the stoop

where he sat thinking of war and of croup 
and the crap
that the bum now was doubtlessly taking 
the mess he was sute to be making
on flowers
 untrimmed and spreading
from alleyways narrow as shoulders
of priests

the sky was shedding its
daylight, picking its molars
and smiling its creased and 
 gummy red smile
bodhis reviled this
pursing his lips against its lewd advances

while across the way,
gently the Virgin 
blue in flickering light
watched courtyard romances

two lovers spilling their beers
their words their seed

She placidly looked on 
in spite of it
just to the right of it
beheld in between and beyond 
what Bodhis could not

squinting and rubbing his belly
in fear of heeding the dyspeptic thought

that in all this grand contumely
the many costumed procession
of man and his making

there was no heart worth having or breaking
no cause for confessions
or cause for aught at all

bodhis thought of this and more 
from his seat on the stoop
in spring's soft pall

while the bum ambled on 
looking for
quieter places
to answer natures call

Text:
"your honor may I approach the bench?"

"you may"
an old dog on a porch, looking up wearily.

time settled around the courtroom, stray coughs.

"your honor," the lawyer whispered, "the evidence--it's admissible, all of it."

"what evidence," said the judge, "none has been presented."

"exhibits A-D, they have strong bearing on my client's case, and attest inconvertibly to his innocence." 

The judge shrugged.

"your honor," the lawyer rubbed his chin, "what would you say to this? I will engage my opponent in a duel, ten paces, him to the [south] and myself to the [north]."

"go on," said the judge, pulling out a toothbrush out from under his robe.

"if," said the lawyer, "I maim but do not kill my opponent, you will acknowledge that (1) there is evidence and (2) it is admissible. If I kill my opponent, you will rule in the favor of my client, innocent of all charges."

"you have no client", said the judge, spreading a thick blue smear of paste.

"furthermore," continued the lawyer, "if my opponent maims but does not kill me, then I will acknowledge that I have no evidence, no client, and that this is not a court of law. if he kills me, well, he will not kill me. I have it on heavenly authority."

the judge gingerly poured water from his glass onto the brush--too much, and it spilled onto his robes. 

"ah, bailiff, napkins please." the judge wiped at the wet spot irritably.

"well, your honor?"

"do as you please," the judge snapped. "I'm not a judge."

"very well," said the lawyer. he walked briskly back to his seat, unclasped his gator skin portmanteau and withdrew a ornate flintlock pistol.

he began polishing the pewter piece as the jury looked on disinterestedly, then one by one disappeared.

this went on for some time, and may very well still be.

Text:
The drumsticks of war 
Beat darkly in the breast
Of the quick brown fox
Coxcombing through his texts
For native notions to behead
Poultry in motion e'er the dead
ones, the fried-in-bread ones
Could raise their ancient cry
Musket powder ink blot
eggfaced crackpots
Pen the henhouse red and
Smoke the peace pipe dry

No fear, 
Chanticleer
for John Smith farms organically
The colors of the wind will be harnessed quite sustainably

And for your bravery
a feathered cap and gown
A thornless crown, 
no cross left to bear
or knee to wound
Or squaws to squabble or prepare your wretched food 

No brooks left to ford
Or arguments left to brook,
Chief among them, Lord, "to take back what was took"
to unbreak that omlette 
unkill mohamet
Uneat the sour grapes
of our hegemonic rapes 
and pillages
(sorry about your villages)

but that's life
And the chicken chicks on chippily
"bookbook"ing while the foxes
Look on licking lippily

musings on antinatalism (to which I am naturally inclined) in light of the idea of "gift" (to which I am unnaturally drawn)

if all life is gift (which in my better moments I believe it be) and to give is better than to recieve (which experience has taught me) then we have a moral obligation to give life, to procreate. however, if all is gift, then so too is the ability to recognize all as gift, and furthermore, it is a gift our offspring may not themselves be given, with suffering generated as a consequence (i.e. the gift refused). So then perhaps withholding-life-from is the superior gift, since it precludes its own refusal.

there is, within each of us, an infinite capacity for silliness, an inexhaustible store of will-to-laugh. this is the great work of the Clown...to inhale the cosmos like helium, to bend and squeak the balloon of being into strange and playful shapes. The work is never done...each goof and gaff gives rise to a thousand thousand more...a joke so senselessly complex, so childishly simple that all the ponderous wisdom of man falls prattishly before it...slipping on discarded peals, ba dum tss, these fibrous fruits of the impish jester god, farting as he goes, flatus ex nihilo...sing, ye fools, of the outre, the absurd, the giddy smear of mummer's paint on all these faceless faces

there is no, can be no, sincerity in an environment where opinion is commodity and  engagement metrics (or buzz) are the prevailing social currency, backed only by empty techoinstitutional promises of "reach," inscribed as and subject to the fickle whims of algorithm. intent is an obsolete standard, nonfungible therefore inconsequential. even measures of approbation (likes/dislike) have less buying power than the gross sum of impressions. this is capital 3.0, erasure of the final vestige of the human interior.

what commonly passes for materialism is actually dualism reskinned, reinforcing the body-mind disjunction by positioning the "natural world" (a loaded and oft misapplied term) as separate from the humans that occupy it. the empirical stance is an inherently adversarial one. the new materialist school (ala Abram and his Husserlian ecophenomologist school) does more to reclaim our lost spiritual heritage than religious institutions while also leaving for scientific innovation/advancement, beckoning toward a new version of an old subject-object synthesis that neither the laboratory nor the chapel can sufficiently accomodate-- that neither the priest nor the scientist has yet to offer-- their respective dogmatics having led to imaginative bankruptcy and ontological isolation of the human from the more-than-human world

lesson 9:
depart, you narrow minded hens
you dealers of inconsequence
get thee hence, dawdlers and dilettantes
and trouble me no more

I'd rather breathe the musty spores
from pages of old books
then smell the smell of flapping lips
and dodge your darting looks
I'd rather feel the wind
or its calm absence
than touch your heated skin
and bear that sentence
that crude brand upon my forehead 
where you kissed me
marred me.

"did you miss me?"

miss? I'd rather die
then miss or be missed by you
I've seen your wares
your wheatless tares
and have no wish to try you

depart! begone!
and leave me to my leavings
infant-like
you gnaw to soothe your carnal teethings
diapered, powdered
toddling yon and thither
or else, cold-eyed and serpentine
you slither

depart
depart, I'd rather die
than listen to your prattling
one moment more;
than be the man stood hat in hand
at your hell-darkened door.
(all the sweaty nothings
traded so cheaply in that place - 
chimeric, emetic, disgrace upon disgrace)

I'd rather die, yes, die,
than be the man who felt you 
and was felt.
I'd rather die
than be the man who smelt you 
and was smelt.

RIVERGIRL
they found her in the bulrushes 
crawling around
a reedy little thing
with sly eyes and sharp teeth
and the sun in her belly

"my daughter of rivers" cooed the barren queen
and stroked her brown cheek 
her tangled hair 
which soon would be shaved in the style of the times,
face painted finely
(all to her great discomfiture)

that was how the river girl
assumed unlikely form 
as terror of the palace;
her list of sins grew long.
many a beleagured tutor or suitor
lost his wig and sandals.
she bit a maid's finger,
defaced the grounds,
pissed once in an ointment pot.

oh but punishments were paltry.
remonstrances, a giggling matter
her eyes played guessing games
feral glints of something hidden, something known,
half-answers to old riddles

her majesty would not feign 
an absent air from royal litters,
but threw her jewelry at the crowds 
with epithets of glee

she portended ill, priests murmured,
cause of a bad harvest, or a plague

but the moon knew otherwise.
waxing full, it watched her 
steal long walks beyond the walls,
pious in the soft night, 
barefoot through riverbeds, 
rich mud clinging 
like a child to a mother

and the crocodiles croaked their prophecies
and the holy bullfrogs chanted

overcome with sanctity,
she whispered prayers to gods that were not hers
halting supplications
arbitrary penances:
	cuts with stones, bites from asps, 
	lying naked on the sharp-hewn stubble

purified in this way, she learned to fly 
with cranes and ibises 
high above snaking sands
to scout entrances to tombs

so that by torchlight, descending
hunched through halls and long-sealed chambers
she might bless them,
the desiccated dead

and so in the night, in the early dawning hours,
the moon watched the rivergirl drift away
knowing the sadness of wisdom and
the coldness of time

the basic precepts of buddhism seem to me to be present in just three of the many (many) intricately braided sets, series, groups, and progressions that comprise its fantastically systematic and numerological doctrines. they are: the four noble truths (life is suffering, suffering is desire, desire can be ended, the way to end it is the eightfold path), the eightfold path (wisdom group: right view, right intention; moral discipline group: right speech, right action, right livelihood; concentration group: right effort, right mindfulness, right concentration) and the defilements (greed, aversion, and delusion, with ignorance at the root)

the actual (and increasingly the stated) purpose of the education system is not to create curious well-informed individuals but a compliant and docile populace. america's rapid "decline" (relative to metrics that "matter") is a direct result of this, i think. collectivism has its merits, on the local level at least, but they become drawbacks at scale. the collective does not invent, it does not innovate, it does not drive growth. That's why communism always becomes authoritarianism; bereft of the engine of individuality, the inert state demands a strong and charismatic hand (which turns quickly to an iron fist). 
if you want to see the origins of the populist movement, look no further than the modern classroom (school as prison, students as inmates. their crime? having been born a self. "oh we will standardize that out of you in no time," say the well-intentioned educators, "we will make you an Average Citizen yet.")
look at their dead vacant eyes, sapped of vitality, dried-out tinder, awaiting the reactionary spark. the conflagration will spread (is spreading) fast and wide, carried on the winds of a nameless anger. 
like all civilizations, we have done this to ourselves, we have created the conditions for our own destruction. it could be no other way.

after a long vague love affair with the idea of eastern philosophy (really a kind of intellectual orientalism), my experience of actually reading Buddhist doctrine is similar to that of going over to a friend's house as a child, convinced that his is the far superior life, until you actually arrive at the Other Place and yourself put off by the strange smells, the strange rules, the alien shadows on the bedroom floor where you lie, listening to your friend snore.

And then, returning afterwards to the home you know, the smells and shadows of long acquaintance; how comforting--that is my experience, afterwards, of turning my mind to Christianity, its old familiar guilts and platitudes...how kind and parental and safe they seem. And how foolish was I to imagine myself better off anywhere else than here!

life is a little idea that climbed into the ear of God. it attached itself and whispered dreams that sproted and budded, climbed out in and around god's head in a great profusion of curling fingers. life covered his arms and legs, filled his insides and found his orifices. a verdant madness, bacchic, tumbling and twining themselves together these many vines formed striated bands which formed limbs, and a trunk, a great green thoughtless body, with the sleeping body of god at with heart. the little idea in the ear of god in the heart of the great green one rejoiced at this and capered and sang a many pitched song, which the  green body danced to, whirling itself through dark reaches and flinging spores, which found their way to other gods, and the lost children of those gods, prodigal and  sleeping in distant places, the pockets of void. Life went there, and seeded them with dreams, filling up the nothing with the tickling growth and leafy density, growing and growing, till every square inch of the vastness infinity was heavy with life and nothing was no more.
life fell on its small back and sighed with relief. yawning, it slept and dreamed an older dream, large and austere...of flint, and hammers, and sparks of a coming cleansing fire.
poem:
life is a little idea that climbed into the ear of God. 
it attached itself and whispered dreams that sproted and budded,
climbed out in and around god's head 
in a great profusion of curling fingers. 
life covered his arms and legs, 
filled his insides and found his orifices. 
a verdant madness, bacchic, 
tumbling and twining themselves together 
these many vines formed striated bands which formed limbs, 
and a trunk, a great green thoughtless body, 
with the sleeping body of god at with heart. 

the little idea in the ear of god 
in the heart of the great green one 
rejoiced at this and capered 
and sang a many pitched song, which the  green body danced to, 
whirling itself through dark reaches and flinging spores, 
which found their way to other gods, 
and the lost children of those gods, prodigal and  sleeping 
in distant places, the pockets of void. 

Life went there, and seeded them with dreams, 
filling up the nothing with the tickling growth 
and leafy density, 
growing and growing, 
till every square inch of the vastness of infinity 
was heavy with life and nothing was no more.

life fell on its small back and sighed with relief. 
yawning, 
it slept and dreamed an older dream, 
large and austere,
of flint, and hammers, 
and sparks of a coming cleansing fire.

Text: the gym is aesthetically, viscerally a horrible place, akin to hell with its clanking weights, its tortured groans, the stench. this is why we must go, not to "get fit," but to prove ourselves to ourselves in the infernal crucible. every afternoon i go, i sit in mt car before in a state of dread. then i grab my wter bottle, i gird my loins, and i descend. i inflict the senseless pain, i undergo the cruel ritual, and when i emerge, it is in a heroic aspect, as a man who has died and lived to tell of it.

ive a slow mind; i'm no great wit. what i would say to young people who think the same of themselves is, who cares. no one is as smart or as dumb as they say or as they feel. allow yourself to become bored, then make the things you are compelled to make out of the boredom. dumb, smart-- make your things, there's not much time; there's all the time in the world.

Text:
a can, warped by the heat of cigarettes dropped in it
the night whistles

norman rockwell painted warm lights
he sits in the mist in February
limning a gospel 
that does not include me

bare trees ache, their nerves exposed to
all of this and more
my twiggy starling
if you can walk around the place enough
the skin will grow over
and the hair will grow on it

and the wind will comb it out
the nits and nests 
into the pool to sink down sodden 
and clog up the drain,
lost continents

for a boy or girl to find, 
inhabited by the ghost of magellan or
the ghost of desoto 

those explorers in hats who
sat a long time in the rusted out deck chairs
waiting for summer
to watch wasps
zig zag 
toward a spot on a map only they can see

I cannot see it now
in the mists of february 
on my balcony overlooking 
all of this and more 

I cannot see it now 
but on my third or fourth time around
walking again past that grimacing tree
or that empty pool
in the mist
in february

I think I can see it

and how it beckons
and how and how

STYX
Out on the river Styx, explained our guide, careering the party along a red road that ran bumpily astride the thicketed bank, through which glimpses of the dark meandering body could here and there be caught, were any number of-- a devastating pothole clacked our teeth-- were any number of historical sights of import, and enjoyable activities for children or adults.

He pointed at a brochure taped overhead. The party shifted nervously, eyeing an oncoming bus, whose driver hailed ours--unheeded--then hurtled past with inches of clearance.  

"River Styx River Water Adventures." Unclear, I thought, how such a bad name could have come to be chosen.

We're nearly there, the driver drawled into the intercom. "We were nearly there 10 minutes ago," one of the party muttered. The others laughed gratefully for the attempt at levity. The driver glared in the rearview.

I turned to a woman with a towel around her, chewing gum, wearing cheap sunglasses with the tag still on. "You believe this?" I asked.

"Oh yeah. You've never been?"

"On the river Styx? No."

"Ah, well it's a fun time."

I looked out at the hot gray sky. "Maybe we'll get some sun finally," I offered.

"Maybe," she declined.

I'd been recently given a coupon for a "tubing experience" on this river I'd never heard of an hour's drive across the state line and--having recently entered one of my not infrequent periods of performative crisis--I decided what the hell. 

A fly in a long-abandoned web--forehead against glass, the metaphor came to me--is what I was. Mine, I knew, was to be a slow, meaningless passing, without even the satisfaction or life-affirming indignity of being a meal for some sultry many-eyed thing who might afterwards call herself my widow. No, impotent and alone, I would wriggle halfheartedly once or twice a year, imagine myself capable of something grand and significant, or at the very least something new, before once again gassing out, quieting down, remembering myself as I was and had been for some two decades now, ensconced in my silken complacency, the secretive little sufferings and comforts of a bachelor and a reasonably happy man. 

The bus squealed. Aaaand everybody out. Out we tumbled, coolers and bags and accouterments in tow. We were instructed to select a "tubular flotation dee-vice" from an unhitched trailer bed on which they were piled. A din of squeaking rubber. Exposed pillbugs rolled and scattered damply. "Look mom," some little bowl-cut terror had climbed up and was stomping them. "Bugs."  

The mother, digging sand from a small clenched mouth, grunted dispassionately. "Down."

Dragging our tubes to the river's edge, there was a grimness in the air, something imminent. The last portage of a fated platoon, death in their eyes. I could see it, the haunting footage of roughnecked men in flip flops and their trailer trash consorts, time-flecked, hollowed-out faces, slow pans set to somber fiddles and the rich intonation of MALE VOICE from every slapped together war-fetish "back then" slop-umentary you've ever seen. Pop. I jumped at a not-so-distant gunshot, sudden diegesis.

"Gun range down the way," said the guide, chuckling past me. He tossed his tube out and leapt onto it, bobbing down and away.

Chastened, I gingerly followed, lowering myself in, hissing as the frigid water touched my ass. God. I read somewhere, a study, that quiet people suffer more to cold and heat, feel more. I took comfort in that.

There was the bowl-cut kid, utterly unaffected by it, splashing in the murk and dunking his brother. "Stop. Hey." Mother already had a beer in hand, watching the Father(?) try to wedge a cooler in a tube. 

Beyond them, I saw the woman in the cheap shades, applying sunscreen on the bank, working it into herself, kneading curves. Should I...? A wave, a beckoning gesture?

But the current had already caught me and I was off, the ones ahead pulling away fast and the ones behind growing small.

I floated for awhile, cold and regretful, missing my chair and my book, earmarked where I'd left it on the sidetable. Another gunshot rang out, closer. 

What would MALE VOICE say of this--two gunshots now--of the lone recruit, deep in unfamiliar territory, adrift on death's own river, "'twixt shrouded banks, bristling and scornful?" Well, what could be said? There was no wartime correspondence, no body of work, no letters to mother or a cherry-lipped beloved--no surviving comrade to inflate with teary testimony my final heroisms. What could be said of him then, of this hapless man floating here, lacking both a battle to fight and a means to fight it?

"'Twixt shrouded banks," I repeated uneasily, making out a sign posted ahead; nailed askew to a fallen tree, it punctuated my vague run-on ruminations, gave form to them, a reptilian one, silhouetted against caution yellow. 

Arching my back, I dabbed nervously, then desperately against the water's pull, willing myself back to center, away from the shaggy overhang, and the things that drop from them, or lurk beneath them--tensed ancient jaws, eyes filmy with instinct.

Overcompensating, I erred toward the opposite bank, avoided it barely too, then back across again--terror, a minute's relief, terror--and in this zig-zag manner I made my way down a fair stretch until at last--wincing, the scratch and crackle of branches--a too-sharp bend left me clinging to a slimy trunk, sucked tight against it. 

Calming somewhat, embarrassed somewhat, I spotted a stick, sensed its potential. Yanking it loose from the snarl, I used it to push free--proud of myself, hesitantly.  

With my makeshift oar, I was able to scull a straight course, growing in confidence enough for my mind to wander back to the woman in shades, inventing unlikely occasions for chivalry: she was drowning, she was stuck, her top had come undone and I retrieved it, eyes gallantly averted. Ridiculous, these fantasies. Did other people have them? Of course. That was the benefit of reading, to be reassured that your sad little dreams were not in fact your own, but the shared property of Man. They gave testament, our books and stories, to the universality of the strange and fragile experience. 

And how fragile, I thought, following the thick oily artery of the river, down, down, past the vignettes of decay and oldblood Carnival on one side and the other: trailers on stilts, sagging and green; scum-lined dinghies docked in stagnant little inlets where cans and styrofoam stippled the shallows; past sandbar revelries, redneck Bacchus cavorting in the company of his Dixieland maenads; past the dark mouths of storm drains--or what I hoped were storm drains--emitting a sulfuric reek.

It went on like this, like a series of stills in rapid succession--time slaking, sloughing itself, bunching up in a whirring projective motion, spooled along this brimstone backwater under sepia skies, wet and mottled, montage disjunction...

[MALE VOICE]

I saw a totem pole strung with telephone lines, flying the Stainless Banner. 

I saw a lawn jockey in a tree stand, defaced like an old god. 

I saw Robert E. Lee in a mobility scooter, cursing, ensnared in kudzu.

I saw, I see myself in ragged grays, a breechloader on my shoulder, hunched and creeping at the flanks--steady men--a sharp flicking past the ears, ffttt, thudding dully an inch off the mark. Blankness, euphoria, then the sick churning of realization, a backward misstep on the scree, tumbling, scrambling, mad animal flight. Ffftt. fffttt. Flickering fast now, silver halide washing out red, the chemical wetness, burnthrough.... a bugle call, distorted, reverberant, then the roar of engagement; men hot for death, marked for it. 

----

A dragonfly landed on my knee, tickling return to present. I smacked at it, looked around. The sun was sinking low; my tube was too, a slow leak from the snag no doubt.

Coming round yet another bend, in the gloaming, I saw the shape of two men in lawn chairs on a point bar, a small lantern at their feet. I clambered out and over to them, my feet sinking deep in the slick pluff of the riverbed. The tube trailed behind me like a limp skin.

"Heya," said one of the men around his dangling cigarette. Approaching him, his [cheekbones] deepened in the lantern light, skeletal. 

"Hey. I'm--" What to even say. "You know where the return point is? For, the river tour. Adventure." 

The man shrugged. "Man, I guess it's a mile back. Or a mile up. You want a cig."

I took one, consigned to the weirdness of it. The lighter flicked.

"So," I took a drag, coughed. Frogs were croaking loudly from the wings in tenuous unison. We watched silently as the last red wound on the western sky congealed and blackened. 

"It's like with Zeus and them." The man said it with authority. His friend remained mute.

"It's like what?" I hadn't smoked in years; it hit me, vertiginous. 

"Like with Zeus and them." He gestured at the river, black and chuckling. "The myths."

Smoke wreathed his face. I groped for something, anything to hang this moment on, a reference point, a precedent. The fissure widened below--yawning, sheer--an infinite strangeness, things slipping away from themselves, caught between banks a universe across, mirrored doubly, trebly, a thousand times over, then shattered at last on the punctum of the bugle's sanguine call.

"I guess it is," I finally said.

ground control to ursa major,
Tom's a minor inconvenience

show the rod and spare no lenience 

said the king of Babylon, his whoring

poring over manuscripts and marginalia?
stick with dynamite and it'll never fail ya

mother's children

Tommy asked the who man

catalytic proselyte
your ruddy hooded ornament
shall not adorn my sleigh tonight

and that's when Tom exhausted pinched the candle light

and so the father and the son
side by side embosom-ed
push up daisies and brasseries
hereafter quite un-cozen-ed

the Energizer bunny beats a reveille
and charges Mother then and there with battery

JERRY SPRINGER

a sensational integrative force. a pillar of sanity and showmanship and good humor, shedding for us a wry, clear light on suppressed and subaltern (un)realities of his time and all time. 
profanities, grotesqueries, perversions: these were his trade and he plied it well, eyebrow permanently raised (and what do you think of THIS dear viewer?), tongue planted firmly in the cheek of a dawning millennium
note if you will how effortlessly he works the room, now from the audience, now from the stage--negotiating, initiating, deescalating--sprezzatura itself--toeing with unflappable poise the line between entertainment and exploitation, between theater and therapy, between man and his shadow

Text:
SEVERANCE
The man at the bar was reasonably certain his dog was trying to communicate with him, and had been for some time. 
"I saw a look in his eye that was human." 
Severance looked over at the man at the bar and shook his head. "That can't be true." 
"He arranges objects in uncanny ways. Errant toys and bones and household items."
"I don't think that's possible." 
"He makes water discreetly and at regular intervals."
"I don't think that's possible." Severance signalled to the bartender. "What's it got to with anything? Passing water."
"He passes water on the hour. It's uncanny."
"That's nonsense. Another beer."
"Come over on Sunday night and I'll show you and you can be the judge."
Severance shrugged and drank in silence. He and the man both stood up at the same time to leave.
***
Sunday night, Severance came around with a bottle of wine. The dog was sleeping on a chair, raised an eye at him. 
"Well I'm here." 
"Yep. This is the dog. Here boy." 
The dog climbed stiffly down with the air of an alderman went to the bookshelf and nosed a volume.
"See that? He's aware and capable." 
 Never mind that.  
 See? These are the archives.  The man thumbed several pages in.  April 15, a message was recieved. Bones of a bird, unmistakably cruciform. Intentional. 
Severance looked at the dog and then the man.  That s nothing.  
 Listen. April 18, another message in bones. 3 lizards, their skeletons carefully articulated. 
 Mm. Where should I put this wine.  Severance went to the kitchen and began rummaging through the empty cabinets.
 Anywhere. Listen. May 1, discovery of an old shed north of the creek. Dilapidated.  The dog began to whine and pace.  Contents included nests, bones. Smell of piss and shit.  
Severance came back with mason jars to pour in.  You never told me you had land. 
 Family land, twenty acres of it. Thank you.  The man sipped.  What kind of name is Severance anyway. 
 Never mind that. Take me to this shed. 
***
It was 9 o clock when they reached it, holding lanterns and high-stepping through the knee-deep kudzu.
 See? That s the place. Here boy.  
The dog barked furiously and ran inside. The men followed to see it pissing in a corner, staring. 
  Left corner, unusual. That could be indicative.  The man took out his notebook.
 You re making something of nothing. You re imposing meaning.  Severance knelt down, picked up an old bottlecap. On the underside, it read,  You have won! 
Like an angel from god, the dog leapt out at him; lantern shattered and the place went up in flames. It took 10 days and crews from three counties to put it out.

kissed a black boy in the cul de sac
I love you now he muttered
through rotten teeth. She sprinted back
to the trailer where her mother sat in silence
picking skin
that was the night the little girl learned the joy of sin
and a love so piteous
that she
returned to pluck the apple knowingly
and pare it down
with a letter opener she found in mother's back
a snack for cerberus,
coyly captioned.
"N-nothing happened" 
she said
then went to bed
drunk on significance.
